docs.title: 🔥 Super Important Interview One-Liner
docs.text: HashSet uniqueness is guaranteed only when equals() and hashCode() follow the contract.

docs.title: Quick check (yes/no)
docs.text: If you override equals() in a custom class, must you override hashCode() also? Why?

docs.title: ✅ Correct Interview Answer
docs.text: Yes. Whenever we override equals(), we must also override hashCode(). Because HashSet and HashMap use hashCode() to determine the bucket and equals() to check equality within that bucket. If we override only equals() and not hashCode(), equal objects may go into different buckets, breaking uniqueness and causing inconsistent behavior.

docs.title: 🔥 Why This Is Critical (Deep Understanding)
docs.text:
HashMap insertion steps:

1️⃣ Calculate hashCode() 2️⃣ Find bucket index 3️⃣ Compare with existing elements using equals()

If two objects are logically equal but have different hash codes:

  • They go into different buckets
  • equals() never gets called
  • Both objects get stored
  • HashSet allows duplicates

docs.title: 🔥 Official Contract (Must Memorize)
docs.text:
If:

a.equals(b) == true

Then:

a.hashCode() == b.hashCode()

Must be true.

But reverse is not required:

  • Same hashCode does NOT mean equals() must be true.

docs.title: 🚀 Now We Move to HashMap Deep Internal Working (Core Interview Topic)
docs.text: We are now entering the most important data structure in Java interviews.

docs.title: 🔹 HashMap Internal Structure (Java 8+)
docs.text:
Internally:

Node<K,V>[] table;

Each bucket contains:

static class Node<K,V> {
    final int hash;
    final K key;
    V value;
    Node<K,V> next;
}

So structure is:

Array of buckets
Each bucket → LinkedList OR Tree

docs.title: 🔥 Step-by-Step: What Happens When You Do
docs.text: map.put(key, value);

docs.title: Step 1️⃣ Compute hash
docs.text: int hash = hash(key);

docs.title: Step 2️⃣ Find index
docs.text:
index = (n - 1) & hash;

(n = table length)

docs.title: Step 3️⃣ Check bucket
docs.text:
Case A: bucket empty → Insert new node.

Case B: bucket not empty → Traverse:

  • If key equal → replace value
  • Else → collision → add node

docs.title: Step 4️⃣ Resize Check
docs.text:
If:

size > capacity * loadFactor

Default:

  • capacity = 16
  • loadFactor = 0.75

Resize occurs.

docs.title: 🔥 Important Question
docs.text:
If initial capacity = 16 loadFactor = 0.75

At what size does resize occur?

Correct answer: Resize happens when size becomes 13 (i.e., after inserting the 13th element).

Let’s calculate properly 👇

docs.title: Given
docs.text:
• Initial capacity = 16
  • Load factor = 0.75

Resize threshold formula:

threshold = capacity × loadFactor

So:

16 × 0.75 = 12

Threshold = 12

docs.title: When Does Resize Actually Trigger?
docs.text:
Resize happens when:

size > threshold

So:

  • After inserting 12 elements → size = 12 → OK
  • When inserting 13th element → size becomes 13 → 13 > 12 → resize

docs.title: ✅ Final Answer
docs.text: Threshold = 12 Resize happens when size becomes 13

docs.title: 🔥 Important Interview Detail
docs.text:
Many people say:

Resize happens at 12.

That is incorrect.

Correct statement:

Resize happens when inserting the 13th element.

docs.title: 🚀 Now Slightly Harder
docs.text:
After resize, what will be new capacity?

Initial capacity was 16.

Answer?

✅ Answer — correct instinct. It becomes double.

docs.title: 🔥 After Resize
docs.text:
Initial:

  • Capacity = 16
  • Threshold = 12

When inserting 13th element → resize triggered.

New capacity:

newCapacity = oldCapacity * 2

So:

16 → 32

docs.title: 🔥 What about new threshold?
docs.text:
Threshold = capacity × loadFactor

32 × 0.75 = 24

So after resize:

  • Capacity = 32
  • Threshold = 24

Resize will happen again when size becomes 25.

docs.title: 🧠 Important Difference from ArrayList
docs.text:
Structure Resize Factor ArrayList 1.5x HashMap 2x

HashMap doubles capacity to reduce collisions.

docs.title: 🚀 Now Very Important Concept (Senior-Level)
docs.text:
When HashMap resizes, does it:

A) Just copy buckets to new array B) Recalculate hash and redistribute entries

✅ Answer — it redistributes entries (rehashes to new buckets). But small refinement: it usually doesn’t “recalculate hashCode()” of the key again; it recomputes bucket index using the stored hash and the new table size.

docs.title: What really happens on HashMap resize (deep)
docs.text:
When capacity doubles (e.g., 16 → 32):

  • HashMap creates a new Node[] table of size 32
  • For each existing entry, it decides the new bucket using:
newIndex = hash & (newCapacity - 1)

Since newCapacity changed, bucket index changes, so entries move.

docs.title: Java 8 optimization (interview bonus)
docs.text:
During resize, Java 8 doesn’t fully recompute everything expensively. It uses a smart trick:

When capacity doubles, each entry either:

  • stays in the same index, or
  • moves to index + oldCapacity

Based on one bit:

(hash & oldCapacity) == 0  → stay
(hash & oldCapacity) != 0  → move to index + oldCapacity

This makes resizing faster.

docs.title: 🧠 Interview one-liner
docs.text: On resize, HashMap redistributes nodes into a larger table because bucket index depends on capacity. In Java 8, entries either stay or move by oldCapacity based on one hash bit.

docs.title: 🔥 Next Critical Question (Collision handling)
docs.text:
In Java 8+, when many keys collide into the same bucket, HashMap may convert the bucket from a LinkedList into a Tree.

  1. What is this conversion called?
  2. After how many nodes (default) does it happen?

docs.title: 1️⃣ Conversion Name
docs.text:
✔ Treeification

When a bucket (collision chain) becomes too long, LinkedList → converted to Red-Black Tree

docs.title: 2️⃣ When does it happen?
docs.text:
✔ When bucket size becomes ≥ 8

More precisely:

  • If bucket size > TREEIFY_THRESHOLD (8)
  • AND table size ≥ 64

Only then treeification happens.

docs.title: 🔥 Important Condition (Many Miss This)
docs.text:
Treeification happens only if:

bucket size ≥ 8
AND
table capacity ≥ 64

If capacity < 64:

  • HashMap will resize instead of treeify.

This avoids unnecessary tree creation.

docs.title: 🧠 Interview One-Liner
docs.text: In Java 8+, if a bucket contains more than 8 nodes and table size is at least 64, it is converted into a Red-Black Tree to improve lookup from O(n) to O(log n).

docs.title: 🚀 Now Very Important Question
docs.text:
What is the time complexity of:

1️⃣ HashMap get() normally 2️⃣ HashMap get() in worst case (before Java 8) 3️⃣ HashMap get() in worst case (after treeification)

✅ Answers:

docs.title: HashMap get() Complexity (Interview-Ready)
docs.text:
1️⃣ Normal average case: O(1)

  • Good hash distribution, small bucket size.

2️⃣ Worst case before Java 8: O(n)

  • All keys collide → one long linked list bucket → linear search.

3️⃣ Worst case after treeification (Java 8+): O(log n)

  • Long bucket becomes Red-Black Tree → log-time lookup.

docs.title: 🔥 Ultra Important Interview Trap: null in HashMap
docs.text:
Answer these:

  1. Does HashMap allow null key?
  2. Does HashMap allow multiple null keys?
  3. How many null keys allowed?
  4. Does HashMap allow null values?

Now let’s go deep because this is a very common interview trap.

docs.title: 1️⃣ Does HashMap allow null key?
docs.text:
✔ Yes

HashMap allows one null key.

docs.title: 2️⃣ Does HashMap allow multiple null keys?
docs.text:
❌ No

Because:

  • Keys must be unique
  • Only one null key allowed

If you insert:

map.put(null, 10);
map.put(null, 20);

Second one replaces the first.

docs.title: 3️⃣ How many null keys allowed?
docs.text: ✔ Only 1

docs.title: 4️⃣ Does HashMap allow null values?
docs.text:
✔ Yes

Multiple null values allowed.

Example:

map.put("A", null);
map.put("B", null);

Perfectly valid.

docs.title: 🔥 Now the Important Internal Question
docs.text:
Where does HashMap store null key internally?

Think carefully.

Since null has no hashCode, what bucket is used?

✅ Answer — it is stored in the first bucket (index 0).

Now let’s explain properly and deeply.

docs.title: 🔥 How HashMap Handles null Key Internally
docs.text:
When you do:

map.put(null, "value");

HashMap logic:

if (key == null)
    return putForNullKey(value);

It does NOT call hashCode().

Instead:

  • It directly inserts into bucket index 0

Why?

Because:

hash(null) = 0
index = (n - 1) & 0 = 0

So null key always goes to bucket 0.

docs.title: 🧠 Important Interview Insight
docs.text: HashMap allows one null key and stores it in bucket 0.

docs.title: 🔥 Now Hard Question (Very Important)
docs.text: Why does HashMap allow null key but Hashtable does NOT?

docs.title: 🔥 Why HashMap allows null but Hashtable does NOT
1️⃣ Design Difference
docs.text:
HashMap

  • Introduced in Java 1.2
  • Not synchronized
  • More flexible
  • Allows 1 null key and multiple null values

Hashtable

  • Legacy class (Java 1.0)
  • Fully synchronized
  • Does NOT allow null key or null value

docs.title: 🔥 The Real Reason (Important)
docs.text:
Hashtable methods are synchronized and older design assumed:

value = table[index];
if (value == null)
    // means key not present

If null values were allowed:

  • Cannot distinguish between:

  • Key not present

  • Key present but value = null

So to avoid ambiguity:

Hashtable does not allow null key or null value.

docs.title: 🔥 Why HashMap allows null then?
docs.text:
HashMap is more modern and internally handles null carefully:

  • Special handling for null key (bucket 0)
  • Null values allowed
  • Uses containsKey() to differentiate

Example:

map.put("A", null);

map.get("A") → null
map.containsKey("A") → true

So HashMap can differentiate.

docs.title: 🧠 Interview One-Liner
docs.text: Hashtable does not allow null keys or values to avoid ambiguity and because of its legacy synchronized design, while HashMap allows one null key and multiple null values.

docs.title: 🚀 Now Very Important Question
docs.text:
Which is thread-safe?

1️⃣ HashMap 2️⃣ Hashtable 3️⃣ ConcurrentHashMap

Correct answer:

docs.title: 3️⃣ ConcurrentHashMap → ✅ Thread-safe (better design)
docs.text: Now let’s explain properly.

docs.title: 🔥 1️⃣ HashMap
docs.text:
• Not synchronized
  • If multiple threads modify it → data corruption possible
  • Can even cause infinite loop during resize (older Java versions)

So:

HashMap → NOT thread-safe

docs.title: 🔥 2️⃣ Hashtable
docs.text:
• All methods are synchronized
  • Only one thread can access at a time
  • Safe but slow

Example:

public synchronized V put(K key, V value)

So:

Hashtable → Thread-safe but inefficient

docs.title: 🔥 3️⃣ ConcurrentHashMap (Most Important)
docs.text:
• Designed for concurrency
  • Not fully synchronized
  • Uses fine-grained locking
  • Allows multiple threads to read/write safely
  • Much faster than Hashtable

So:

ConcurrentHashMap → Thread-safe and efficient

docs.title: 🧠 Interview One-Liner
docs.text: HashMap is not thread-safe. Hashtable is thread-safe but fully synchronized and slow. ConcurrentHashMap is thread-safe with better performance using segmented locking (Java 7) or CAS + synchronized bins (Java 8+).

docs.title: 🚀 Now Very Important Question
docs.text:
Does ConcurrentHashMap allow null key or null value?

Think carefully. This is tricky.

✅ Answer — ConcurrentHashMap does NOT allow null key or null value.

Now let’s understand why (very important for interviews).

docs.title: 🔥 Why ConcurrentHashMap does NOT allow null?
docs.text:
In concurrent environment:

If this was allowed:

map.put("A", null);

Now if another thread does:

map.get("A");

It returns null.

Now ambiguity:

  • Is key not present?
  • Or key present but value is null?

In single-threaded HashMap, you can call:

map.containsKey("A");

But in concurrent environment:

Between get() and containsKey():

  • Another thread may remove or modify entry.
  • So result becomes inconsistent.

To avoid this ambiguity:

ConcurrentHashMap does not allow null keys or values.

docs.title: 🧠 Interview One-Liner
docs.text: ConcurrentHashMap does not allow null keys or values because in concurrent environments null creates ambiguity between “key absent” and “value null”.

docs.title: 🔥 Quick Comparison Table
docs.text: Map Type Thread-Safe Null Key Null Value HashMap ❌ No ✅ 1 ✅ Yes Hashtable ✅ Yes ❌ No ❌ No ConcurrentHashMap ✅ Yes ❌ No ❌ No

docs.title: 🚀 Now Important Concept: Fail-Fast
docs.text:
What happens if you modify an ArrayList while iterating using for-each loop?

Example:

ArrayList<Integer> list = new ArrayList<>();
list.add(1);
list.add(2);
list.add(3);

for (Integer i : list) {
    list.remove(i);
}

What happens?

✅ Answer: — it throws:

ConcurrentModificationException

Now let’s go deep and make it interview-ready.

docs.title: Why ConcurrentModificationException Happens?
docs.text:
When you iterate using:

for (Integer i : list)

Internally it uses an Iterator:

Iterator<Integer> it = list.iterator();

ArrayList maintains:

modCount

Every structural modification:

  • add()
  • remove()
  • clear()

Increments modCount.

docs.title: What Iterator Stores
docs.text:
Iterator stores:

expectedModCount

During iteration:

if (modCount != expectedModCount)
    throw ConcurrentModificationException;

So when you modify list directly:

list.remove(i);

modCount changes But iterator’s expectedModCount does not → mismatch → exception thrown

docs.title: 🔥 Important Interview Concept
docs.text:
This is called:

Fail-Fast behavior

Fail-fast means:

  • Detects structural modification
  • Fails immediately
  • Does not allow unpredictable behavior

docs.title: 🧠 Interview One-Liner
docs.text: ArrayList iterators are fail-fast. If the collection is structurally modified after the iterator is created (except through iterator.remove()), it throws ConcurrentModificationException.

docs.title: 🚀 Now Very Important Question
docs.text: How can you safely remove elements while iterating?

docs.title: ✅ Correct & Recommended Approach (Interview Answer)
docs.text:
Use Iterator.remove()

Iterator<Integer> it = list.iterator();
while (it.hasNext()) {
    Integer val = it.next();
    if (val % 2 == 0) {
        it.remove();  // SAFE
    }
}

Why safe?

Because:

  • it.remove() updates both

  • modCount

  • expectedModCount
  • So no mismatch

docs.title: 🔥 Java 8+ Best Way
docs.text:
Use:

list.removeIf(val -> val % 2 == 0);

Internally safe.

docs.title: 🧠 Interview One-Liner
docs.text: To safely remove elements while iterating, use Iterator.remove() or removeIf(). Modifying the collection directly during iteration causes ConcurrentModificationException.

docs.title: 🚀 Now Important Question
docs.text:
Are HashMap iterators fail-fast?

Example:

for (String key : map.keySet()) {
    map.remove(key);
}

Will this throw exception?

✅ Yes, it will throw ConcurrentModificationException (in normal HashMap).

docs.title: Why?
docs.text:
HashMap iterators are also fail-fast, just like ArrayList.

When you iterate:

for (String key : map.keySet())

Internally it uses an iterator over the keySet view, and HashMap also maintains a modification count (modCount).

If you do:

map.remove(key);

That’s a structural modification while iterating → modCount changes → iterator detects mismatch → throws ConcurrentModificationException.

docs.title: ✅ Correct ways to remove while iterating a HashMap
1️⃣ Use iterator.remove()
docs.text:
Iterator<String> it = map.keySet().iterator();
while (it.hasNext()) {
    String key = it.next();
    if (/* condition */) {
        it.remove(); // safe
    }
}

docs.title: 2️⃣ Use removeIf on entrySet (Java 8+)
docs.text: map.entrySet().removeIf(e -> /* condition */);

docs.title: 🧠 Interview One-Liner
docs.text: HashMap iterators are fail-fast. Modifying the map structurally during iteration (except via iterator.remove()) causes ConcurrentModificationException.

docs.title: 🔥 Next: TreeSet / TreeMap Core Concept
docs.text:
Before we move there, answer this:

What is the difference between HashMap and TreeMap in terms of ordering and internal data structure? (2–3 lines)

HashMap not maintain sorting order but TreeMap does. Tree map maintains sorted order (based on natural ordering or Comparator). May be HashMap internally use arrays to store bucket and TreeMap use tree data structure.

Now let’s make it interview-perfect.

docs.title: HashMap
docs.text:
• Does NOT maintain insertion order
  • Does NOT maintain sorted order
  • Order is unpredictable

docs.title: TreeMap
docs.text:
• Maintains sorted order

  • Sorting based on:

  • Natural ordering (Comparable)

  • OR custom Comparator

docs.title: HashMap
docs.text:
• Uses:

  • Array of buckets

  • LinkedList (before Java 8)
  • Red-Black Tree (if bucket size ≥ 8)

So it's primarily hash-based.

docs.title: TreeMap
docs.text:
• Uses Red-Black Tree
  • Self-balancing binary search tree
  • All operations O(log n)

docs.title: 🔥 Time Complexity Comparison
docs.text: Operation HashMap TreeMap get() O(1) average O(log n) put() O(1) average O(log n) remove() O(1) average O(log n) Ordering No Sorted

docs.title: 🧠 Interview One-Liner
docs.text: HashMap is hash-based and provides O(1) average time complexity without ordering guarantees. TreeMap is a Red-Black Tree that maintains sorted order with O(log n) operations.

docs.title: 🚀 Now Important Concept
docs.text:
TreeMap requires keys to be:

1️⃣ Comparable OR 2️⃣ Provided with Comparator

Why?

What happens if keys are not comparable?

Correct answer:

👉 It will throw ClassCastException at runtime.

docs.title: Why?
docs.text:
TreeMap uses a Red-Black Tree.

To insert a key, it must compare keys:

Either using:

Comparable.compareTo()

OR

Comparator.compare()

If keys are not comparable, TreeMap cannot determine:

  • left child
  • right child

So it fails during insertion.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
class Person {
    int id;
}

TreeMap<Person, String> map = new TreeMap<>();
map.put(new Person(), "A");

This will throw:

java.lang.ClassCastException

Because Person does not implement Comparable and no Comparator provided.

docs.title: 🧠 Interview One-Liner
docs.text: TreeMap requires keys to be comparable. If keys are not comparable and no Comparator is provided, it throws ClassCastException at runtime.

docs.title: 🔥 Now Deep Question
docs.text:
What is difference between:

1️⃣ Comparable 2️⃣ Comparator

Explain in 3–4 lines. This is very common interview question.

Comparable: It is a interface provide a compare method to decide natural sort order.
Comparator you can use explicity to sort the collection.

Now let’s make it interview-perfect + deeper.

docs.title: 1️⃣ Comparable
docs.text:
• Package: java.lang
  • Method:
int compareTo(T o);
  • Defines natural ordering
  • Class itself implements it
  • Only one natural order possible

Example:

class Person implements Comparable<Person> {
    int age;

    public int compareTo(Person p) {
        return this.age - p.age;
    }
}

Now:

Collections.sort(list);

Uses compareTo()

docs.title: 2️⃣ Comparator
docs.text:
• Package: java.util
  • Method:
int compare(T o1, T o2);
  • Used for custom ordering
  • Separate class or lambda
  • Multiple sorting strategies possible

Example:

Comparator<Person> byName = (p1, p2) -> p1.name.compareTo(p2.name);
Collections.sort(list, byName);

docs.title: 🔥 Key Differences Table
docs.text: Feature Comparable Comparator Package java.lang java.util Method compareTo() compare() Defined in Same class Separate class Natural order Yes No Multiple sorting ❌ Only one ✅ Multiple

docs.title: 🧠 Interview One-Liner
docs.text: Comparable defines the natural ordering of objects within the class, while Comparator defines custom ordering externally and allows multiple sorting strategies.

docs.title: 🚀 Now Important Edge Case
docs.text:
If compareTo() returns 0, what does that mean in:

1️⃣ TreeSet 2️⃣ TreeMap

Think carefully — this is important.

Correct answer:

If compareTo() returns 0, it means:

The two objects are considered equal in terms of ordering.

And in TreeSet / TreeMap, that means:

👉 The second element will NOT be inserted.

docs.title: 🔥 Why?
docs.text:
TreeSet and TreeMap use:

  • compareTo() (or Comparator)
  • NOT equals() to determine uniqueness

So if:

obj1.compareTo(obj2) == 0

TreeSet treats them as duplicates — even if equals() returns false.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
class Person implements Comparable<Person> {
    int age;
    String name;

    public int compareTo(Person p) {
        return this.age - p.age;  // only age considered
    }
}

Now:

TreeSet<Person> set = new TreeSet<>();

set.add(new Person(25, "A"));
set.add(new Person(25, "B"));

Since compareTo returns 0 (same age):

Second object is NOT added.

Even though names differ.

docs.title: 🧠 Critical Interview Rule
docs.text: In TreeSet and TreeMap, uniqueness is determined by compareTo() (or Comparator), not equals().

docs.title: 🚀 Now Deep Question
docs.text:
If equals() says two objects are equal, but compareTo() does NOT return 0,

what happens in TreeSet?

✅ Answer:

If:

  • equals() returns true
  • but compareTo() does NOT return 0

👉 Both objects will be inserted into TreeSet.

docs.title: 🔥 Why?
docs.text:
Because:

TreeSet uses:

compareTo() OR Comparator

to determine:

  • Order
  • Uniqueness

It does NOT use equals() to prevent duplicates.

So if:

obj1.equals(obj2) == true

But:

obj1.compareTo(obj2) != 0

TreeSet thinks they are different.

docs.title: 🚨 Important Rule (Very Important for Interviews)
docs.text:
For sorted collections:

compareTo() returning 0 defines equality, not equals().

docs.title: 🔥 Why This Is Dangerous?
docs.text:
Because this violates the general contract:

Java recommends:

if a.equals(b) == true
then a.compareTo(b) should return 0

If not:

  • You may get inconsistent behavior
  • Collections behave unexpectedly

docs.title: 🧠 Interview One-Liner
docs.text: In TreeSet and TreeMap, equality is determined by compareTo() or Comparator. If compareTo() is inconsistent with equals(), it may allow logical duplicates.

docs.title: 🚀 We Have Covered:
docs.text:
• ArrayList internals
  • LinkedList internals
  • HashSet internals
  • HashMap internals
  • Resize logic
  • Treeification
  • Fail-fast
  • Null behavior
  • TreeMap internals
  • Comparable vs Comparator
  • Uniqueness rules

You are now at strong intermediate-to-senior level on Collections.

docs.title: 📚 COLLECTIONS FRAMEWORK – Part-2
docs.text:
Following topics we will conver in this part:

1️⃣ Time Complexity Master Table (Complete Revision Map) 2️⃣ Red-Black Tree Basics (for TreeMap understanding) 3️⃣ ConcurrentHashMap Internals (Deep concurrency insight) 4️⃣ Generics Deep Dive (PECS, Wildcards, Type Erasure)

We start with Phase 1.

docs.title: 🔥 PHASE 1 — MASTER TIME COMPLEXITY TABLE (Interview Gold)
docs.text: You must know this without thinking.

docs.title: 1️⃣ List Implementations
docs.text:
Operation ArrayList LinkedList add(e) O(1)* O(1) add(index, e) O(n) O(n) get(index) O(1) O(n) remove(index) O(n) O(n) removeFirst() O(n) O(1) removeLast() O(1) O(1) contains() O(n) O(n) Iteration O(n) O(n)
  • O(1) amortized.

docs.title: 2️⃣ Set Implementations
docs.text:
Operation HashSet LinkedHashSet TreeSet add() O(1)* O(1)* O(log n) remove() O(1)* O(1)* O(log n) contains() O(1)* O(1)* O(log n) Ordering No Insertion Sorted Null allowed Yes Yes No (if using natural ordering)
  • average case.

docs.title: 3️⃣ Map Implementations
docs.text: Operation HashMap LinkedHashMap TreeMap ConcurrentHashMap put() O(1)* O(1)* O(log n) O(1)* get() O(1)* O(1)* O(log n) O(1)* remove() O(1)* O(1)* O(log n) O(1)* Ordering No Insertion Sorted No Thread-safe No No No Yes Null key 1 1 No No

docs.title: 4️⃣ Worst Case Summary
docs.text: Structure Worst Case HashMap (pre-Java 8) O(n) HashMap (Java 8+) O(log n) TreeMap O(log n) ArrayList add O(n) (resize) LinkedList get O(n)

docs.title: 🔥 Important Pattern Recognition
docs.text:
Hash-based structures:

  • Fast average
  • Depend on hashCode quality

Tree-based structures:

  • Always O(log n)
  • Ordered

Array-based structures:

  • Fast random access
  • Expensive shifting

Linked structures:

  • Fast head/tail ops
  • Slow random access

docs.title: 🚀 Now Small Rapid-Fire Test
docs.text:
Answer without thinking too much:

1️⃣ Fastest for search? (Average case) 2️⃣ Best for sorted data? 3️⃣ Best for LRU cache base? 4️⃣ Best for concurrent high-read workload? 5️⃣ Best for frequent head insertions?

Answer 1 line each.

docs.title: 1️⃣ Fastest for search (average case)?
docs.text: ✔ Hash-based structures (HashMap / HashSet) Because average time complexity is O(1).

docs.title: 2️⃣ Best for sorted data?
docs.text: ✔ TreeMap / TreeSet Because they maintain sorted order using Red-Black Tree.

docs.title: 3️⃣ Best for LRU cache base?
docs.text:
You said: “Maybe HashBased” → ❌ Not precise.

✔ Correct answer: LinkedHashMap

Why?

LinkedHashMap:

  • Maintains insertion order
  • Can maintain access order
  • Supports overriding:
protected boolean removeEldestEntry()

Used internally to build LRU cache.

Interview one-liner:

LinkedHashMap is best for implementing LRU cache because it maintains access order.

docs.title: 4️⃣ Best for concurrent high-read workload?
docs.text:
✔ ConcurrentHashMap

Because:

  • Thread-safe
  • Allows high concurrency
  • Uses fine-grained locking / CAS

docs.title: 5️⃣ Best for frequent head insertions?
docs.text:
✔ LinkedList

Because:

  • O(1) insertion at head
  • No shifting

docs.title: 🔥 Summary: You’re thinking in correct direction.
docs.text:
Only LRU needed correction.

Now we go deeper.

docs.title: 🚀 PHASE 2 — RED-BLACK TREE BASICS
docs.text:
This is important for:

  • TreeMap
  • TreeSet
  • HashMap treeification

docs.title: 1️⃣ What is Red-Black Tree?
docs.text:
It is a:

Self-balancing Binary Search Tree

Guarantees:

Height ≈ log n

So operations:

insert → O(log n)
delete → O(log n)
search → O(log n)

docs.title: 2️⃣ Why Not Normal BST?
docs.text:
Normal BST can become:

1
 \
  2
   \
    3
     \
      4

Height becomes n Operations become O(n)

Red-Black Tree prevents that.

docs.title: 3️⃣ Core Red-Black Rules (Memorize)
docs.text:
1️⃣ Every node is either RED or BLACK 2️⃣ Root is always BLACK 3️⃣ No two RED nodes adjacent 4️⃣ Every path from node to null has same number of BLACK nodes 5️⃣ Null leaves are BLACK

These rules maintain balance.

docs.title: 4️⃣ Why TreeMap Uses Red-Black Tree?
docs.text:
Because:

  • Predictable O(log n)
  • Self-balancing
  • Efficient for sorted maps

docs.title: 🔥 Interview Insight
docs.text:
When HashMap bucket size ≥ 8:

  • LinkedList → converted to Red-Black Tree
  • Improves from O(n) → O(log n)

docs.title: ✅ Which is faster for small data size?
docs.text: ✔ HashMap

docs.title: Why?
docs.text:
• Average time complexity = O(1)
  • No tree balancing overhead
  • Lower constant factors

TreeMap:

  • Always O(log n)
  • Has Red-Black Tree rotations
  • More pointer operations

So for small and unsorted data:

HashMap is generally faster.

docs.title: 🔥 Important Insight
docs.text:
Even though HashMap worst-case is O(log n) (Java 8+), TreeMap always remains O(log n).

So unless you need sorted order:

Prefer HashMap.

docs.title: 🚀 Now PHASE 3 — ConcurrentHashMap Deep Internals
docs.text: This is advanced and very important for backend interviews.

docs.title: 1️⃣ Why Not Just Use HashMap in Multi-thread?
docs.text:
If multiple threads modify HashMap:

  • Data corruption
  • Lost updates
  • Infinite loop during resize (pre-Java 8)
  • Race conditions

So not safe.

docs.title: 2️⃣ How Hashtable Solves It?
docs.text:
• Entire methods synchronized

Example:

public synchronized V put(K key, V value)

Problem:

  • Only one thread at a time
  • Low performance

docs.title: Java 7 approach:
docs.text:
• Segmented locking
  • Divides map into segments
  • Lock only segment

docs.title: Java 8+ approach:
docs.text:
• No segments

  • Uses:

  • CAS (Compare-And-Swap)

  • synchronized only on bucket level

So:

  • Multiple threads can operate on different buckets simultaneously

docs.title: 🔥 Important Differences
docs.text: Feature HashMap ConcurrentHashMap Thread-safe No Yes Locking None Fine-grained Null allowed Yes No Resize safe No Yes

docs.title: 🔥 Key Concept: CAS
docs.text:
CAS = Compare And Swap

Used for:

  • Lock-free updates
  • High concurrency

docs.title: 🚀 Interview-Level Question
docs.text:
Is ConcurrentHashMap completely lock-free?

✅ Answer — No, ConcurrentHashMap is not completely lock-free.

Now let’s explain properly at interview level.

docs.title: 🔥 Is ConcurrentHashMap completely lock-free?
docs.text:
👉 No.

It uses a combination of:

  • CAS (Compare-And-Swap) for some operations
  • Synchronized blocks (on bins/buckets) for others

So it is:

Partially lock-free, but not fully lock-free.

docs.title: 🔍 What is Lock-Free Here?
docs.text:
For example:

  • When inserting into an empty bucket
  • It uses CAS to set the value

No lock needed.

docs.title: 🔒 When Does It Use Lock?
docs.text:
If:

  • Bucket already contains elements
  • Collision happens
  • Tree bin modification

Then it uses:

synchronized (bucketNode) {
   // modify bucket
}

But only that specific bucket is locked, not whole map.

docs.title: 🔥 Why This Is Powerful?
docs.text:
Because:

  • Different threads can modify different buckets simultaneously.
  • Very high concurrency compared to Hashtable.

docs.title: 🧠 Interview One-Liner
docs.text: ConcurrentHashMap is not fully lock-free. It uses CAS for simple operations and synchronized blocks at bucket level for collision handling, providing high concurrency without full map locking.

docs.title: 🚀 Now Important Question
docs.text:
Does ConcurrentHashMap allow:

1️⃣ Concurrent reads without locking? 2️⃣ Concurrent writes on different buckets?

docs.title: 1️⃣ Concurrent reads without locking?
docs.text:
✔ Yes

ConcurrentHashMap allows:

  • Multiple threads to read simultaneously
  • No locking required for read operations (in most cases)

Why?

  • It uses volatile variables
  • Proper memory visibility guarantees
  • Reads do not block writes

docs.title: 2️⃣ Concurrent writes on different buckets?
docs.text:
✔ Yes

Because:

  • Locking happens at bucket level (Java 8+)
  • Different threads modifying different buckets
  • No interference

This gives high concurrency.

docs.title: 🔥 Important Clarification
docs.text:
If two threads modify the same bucket:

  • Then synchronization happens
  • Only one thread modifies that bucket at a time

But other buckets remain free.

docs.title: 🧠 Interview One-Liner
docs.text: ConcurrentHashMap allows concurrent reads and concurrent writes on different buckets using fine-grained locking and CAS, improving performance over Hashtable.

docs.title: 🚀 Now PHASE 4 — GENERICS (Deep + Senior-Level)
docs.text: This is extremely important.

docs.title: 1️⃣ Why Generics?
docs.text:
Before Java 5:

```java id="zpmv0t" List list = new ArrayList(); list.add("A"); list.add(10); 

Problem:

* No type safety
* ClassCastException at runtime

Generics introduced:

* Compile-time type safety
* No casting needed

---

# 2️⃣ Basic Syntax

```java id="1k0msp"
List<String> list = new ArrayList<>();

Now:

  • Only String allowed
  • Compile-time safety

docs.title: 3️⃣ What is Type Erasure?
docs.text:
Generics exist only at compile-time.

At runtime:

```java id="h5bivv" List 

Becomes:

```java id="cjj3cn"
List

Type information erased.

So:

List<String>.class

Does NOT exist.

docs.title: 🔥 Important Consequence
docs.text:
You cannot do:

```java id="xok5qq" if (obj instanceof List) 

Because generics erased at runtime.

---

# 🚀 Now Important Question

Can you create:

```java id="sdntm1"
List<int> list = new ArrayList<>();

Why or why not?

✅ Answer: No, we can not create using primitive types.

> Generics work only with reference types because of type erasure. Primitive types like int must be wrapped in their corresponding wrapper classes like Integer.

Small correction:

  • Wrapper classes like Integer do not extend Object directly, they extend Number, and Number extends Object.

Now let’s give the perfect answer.

docs.title: ✅ Why List<int> is Not Allowed?
docs.text:
List<int> list = new ArrayList<>(); ❌

Because:

1️⃣ Generics work only with reference types 2️⃣ int is a primitive type, not a reference type 3️⃣ Generics use type erasure, and primitives cannot be used in type parameters

So you must use:

List<Integer> list = new ArrayList<>();

Java automatically handles autoboxing:

list.add(10);   // int → Integer

docs.title: 🔥 Important Interview Insight
docs.text:
Why generics don’t support primitives?

Because internally after type erasure:

List<Integer>

Becomes:

List

And everything is treated as Object.

But primitive types are not objects.

docs.title: 🧠 Interview One-Liner
docs.text: Generics work only with reference types because of type erasure. Primitive types like int must be wrapped in their corresponding wrapper classes like Integer.

docs.title: 🚀 Now We Go Deeper — Wildcards
docs.text:
Explain the difference between:

List<?>
List<Object>
List<? extends Number>
List<? super Integer>

We go step-by-step.

docs.title: ✅ What is List<?> ?
docs.text:
Correct meaning:

List<?> means a list of unknown type.

It does NOT mean “any reference item allowed to add”.

It means:

  • It could be List<String>
  • Or List<Integer>
  • Or List<Person>
  • We don’t know which.

docs.title: 🔥 Important Rule
docs.text:
With List<?>:

✔ You can read elements as Object ❌ You cannot add elements (except null)

Example:

List<?> list = new ArrayList<String>();

Object obj = list.get(0);   // allowed

list.add("Hello");  // ❌ Compile error
list.add(null);     // ✅ allowed

docs.title: Why can’t we add?
docs.text:
Because compiler does not know actual type.

If:

List<?> list = new ArrayList<Integer>();

Then:

list.add("Hello");  // would break type safety

So Java blocks all additions (except null).

docs.title: 🧠 Interview One-Liner
docs.text: List<?> represents a list of unknown type. You can read elements as Object, but you cannot add elements except null.

docs.title: 🚀 Now Next Level
docs.text:
What is the difference between:

List<Object>

and

List<?>

Think carefully. This is very important.

List<?> it define list is any kind of similar type. So basically you don't know the type here. SO you cannot add elements here because type safety.
But List<Object> is define list is object type. It contains object which may or may not similar types.
It will allow add method. Here get method return element as object

Let me refine it into a perfect interview-level explanation.

docs.title: 1️⃣ List<Object>
docs.text:
List<Object> list = new ArrayList<>();

Meaning:

  • This list can store any object type
  • Because everything in Java extends Object

You can:

list.add("Hello");
list.add(10);
list.add(new Person());

✔ You can add elements ✔ get() returns Object

Important:

List<String> is NOT a subtype of List<Object>

This is very important.

List<String> strList = new ArrayList<>();
List<Object> objList = strList;  // ❌ Compile error

Generics are invariant.

docs.title: 2️⃣ List<?>
docs.text:
List<?> list = new ArrayList<String>();

Meaning:

  • A list of unknown type
  • Could be List
  • Could be List
  • Compiler does not know

    • So:

    ✔ You can read as Object ❌ You cannot add elements (except null)

docs.title: 🔥 Key Difference Summary
docs.text: Feature List

docs.title: 📚 Multithreading – Part-1
docs.text: Multithreading deep dive, end-to-end. We’ll go basic → advanced, with internals + traps + interview answers. No fluff.

docs.title: Level 1: What is a Thread? What is a Process?
docs.text:
• Process: independent program instance (separate memory space).
  • Thread: execution path inside a process (threads share heap, have separate stacks).

Key interview line

Threads share heap (objects) but each thread has its own stack (local variables, frames).

docs.title: Option A: Extend Thread
docs.text:
class T extends Thread {
  public void run() { System.out.println("run"); }
}
new T().start();

docs.title: Option B: Implement Runnable (preferred)
docs.text:
class Task implements Runnable {
  public void run() { System.out.println("run"); }
}
new Thread(new Task()).start();

Why Runnable preferred?

  • Your class can extend something else (Java single inheritance)
  • Separation of task (Runnable) from execution (Thread)

docs.title: ✅ Absolute Must-Know: start() vs run() (Top trap)
docs.text:
• start() → creates a new OS/JVM thread, then calls run() on that new thread.
  • run() → normal method call, runs on current thread, no new thread.

Interview one-liner

Calling run() doesn’t start a new thread; start() does.

docs.title: Thread Lifecycle (Interview)
docs.text:
States (simplified):

  • NEW
  • RUNNABLE (ready/running)
  • BLOCKED (waiting for monitor lock)
  • WAITING (wait/join without timeout)
  • TIMED_WAITING (sleep/join with timeout)
  • TERMINATED

docs.title: CPU Scheduling Basics (What you must accept)
docs.text:
• Thread execution order is not guaranteed
  • Prints can interleave unpredictably
  • Priority is a hint, not a guarantee (OS-dependent)

docs.title: Q1: start vs run
docs.text:
class A extends Thread {
  public void run() { System.out.print("R"); }
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    A t = new A();
    t.run();
    System.out.print("M");
  }
}

What prints?

docs.title: Q2: start order
docs.text:
class A extends Thread {
  public void run() { System.out.print("T"); }
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    new A().start();
    System.out.print("M");
  }
}

Possible outputs?

docs.title: Q3: sleep
docs.text:
public class Test {
  public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
    System.out.print("A");
    Thread.sleep(0);
    System.out.print("B");
  }
}

What prints?

Reply with:

  • Q1 output
  • Q2 possible outputs
  • Q3 output

The correct answers + why (deep, interview style).

docs.title: Q1: run() vs start()
docs.text:
Code:

t.run();
System.out.print("M");

docs.title: ✅ Output
docs.text: RM

docs.title: Why
docs.text: t.run() is just a normal method call on the main thread, so it prints R, then main prints M.

docs.title: Q2: start() scheduling
docs.text:
Code:

new A().start();
System.out.print("M");

docs.title: ✅ Possible outputs
docs.text:
TM
MT

docs.title: Why
docs.text:
start() creates a new thread, but scheduling is unpredictable:

  • Thread may run first → T then M
  • Main may print first → M then T

docs.title: Q3: Thread.sleep(0)
docs.text:
Code:

System.out.print("A");
Thread.sleep(0);
System.out.print("B");

docs.title: ✅ Output
docs.text: AB

docs.title: Why
docs.text: sleep(0) does not end the thread or switch threads reliably. It just sleeps for 0 ms (effectively no-op). So prints A then B.

docs.title: ✅ Quick Fix: Your mistakes
docs.text:
• Q1: you missed that run() prints R
  • Q3: you confused print character (B) with M

docs.title: What is a race condition?
docs.text: When multiple threads access shared data and the result depends on timing.

docs.title: 🔥 Trap 4: Predict output (very common)
docs.text:
class Counter {
  int count = 0;
  void inc() { count++; }
}

public class Test {
  public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
    Counter c = new Counter();

    Thread t1 = new Thread(() -> { for(int i=0;i<100000;i++) c.inc(); });
    Thread t2 = new Thread(() -> { for(int i=0;i<100000;i++) c.inc(); });

    t1.start(); t2.start();
    t1.join();  t2.join();

    System.out.println(c.count);
  }
}

Question:

  1. Will it always print 200000?
  2. If not, why? (one key reason)

Answer:

count++ is not atomic. It consists of read–modify–write operations, so multiple threads may read the same value and overwrite each other's updates, causing a race condition.

Now let’s explain properly.

docs.title: 1️⃣ Will it always print 200000?
docs.text: ❌ No

docs.title: 2️⃣ Why?
docs.text:
Because of a race condition.

The operation:

count++;

is not atomic.

It actually performs three steps internally.

docs.title: Internal Working of count++
docs.text:
1️⃣ Read value from memory
2️⃣ Add 1
3️⃣ Write value back

Example scenario:

Initial value:

count = 5

Thread execution:

Step Thread 1 Thread 2 Read 5 5 Add 6 6 Write 6 6

Final value:

6

But two increments happened, so expected value should be 7.

One increment is lost.

This is called:

docs.title: Interview One-Liner
docs.text: count++ is not atomic. It consists of read–modify–write operations, so multiple threads may read the same value and overwrite each other's updates, causing a race condition.

docs.title: Option 1: synchronized
docs.text:
synchronized void inc() {
    count++;
}

docs.title: Option 2: AtomicInteger (better)
docs.text:
AtomicInteger count = new AtomicInteger(0);

count.incrementAndGet();

This uses CAS (Compare-And-Swap).

docs.title: Next Important Topic (Very Important)
docs.text: Before moving ahead, answer this.

docs.title: Trap Question
docs.text:
What is the difference between:

Thread.sleep()

and

Object.wait()

Give 2 differences. This is a very common interview question.

docs.title: sleep()
docs.text:
• It is a static method of Thread.
  • You call it as:
Thread.sleep(1000);

docs.title: wait()
docs.text:
• It is a method of Object.
  • You call it on a lock object:
lock.wait();

docs.title: sleep()
docs.text:
❌ Does NOT release any lock.

If a thread is inside a synchronized block and calls sleep, it keeps the lock.

docs.title: wait()
docs.text:
✅ Releases the lock (monitor) immediately and goes to waiting state.

This is the biggest difference.

docs.title: sleep()
docs.text:
• Can be called anywhere.
  • No special requirement.

docs.title: wait()
docs.text:
• Must be called inside synchronized context (owning the monitor of that object).
  • Otherwise you get:
IllegalMonitorStateException

Example (wrong):

lock.wait(); // ❌ if not inside synchronized(lock)

Correct:

synchronized(lock) {
    lock.wait(); // ✅
}

docs.title: 4) How does a thread wake up?
sleep()
docs.text:
• Wakes up automatically after time ends (or interrupt).
  • No other thread needs to do anything.

docs.title: wait()
docs.text:
• Wakes up if:

  • another thread calls notify() / notifyAll() on same lock object OR

  • timeout ends (wait(ms)) OR
  • interrupted

docs.title: sleep()
docs.text:
• Pause thread for some time.
  • Rate limiting, retry delay, polling delay.

docs.title: wait()
docs.text:
• Thread coordination / communication.
  • Classic producer-consumer pattern.

docs.title: ✅ Short Interview Answer (Memorize)
docs.text: sleep() pauses the current thread without releasing any locks and can be called anywhere. wait() is used for inter-thread communication; it must be called inside a synchronized block and it releases the lock until notified or timed out.

docs.title: 🔥 Quick Trap (to confirm understanding)
docs.text:
Predict what happens (no need exact output, just behavior):

Object lock = new Object();

Thread t1 = new Thread(() -> {
  synchronized(lock) {
    try {
      Thread.sleep(2000);
    } catch (Exception e) {}
  }
});

Thread t2 = new Thread(() -> {
  synchronized(lock) {
    System.out.println("t2 entered");
  }
});

t1.start();
Thread.sleep(100);
t2.start();

Question: Will t2 print immediately or after ~2 seconds? Why?

Answer: 

Thread.sleep() pauses the thread but keeps the monitor lock, while wait() releases the lock and allows other threads to acquire it.

Now let’s understand deeply.

docs.title: Correct Behavior
docs.text:
t2 will print after about 2 seconds, not immediately.

Output (after ~2s):

t2 entered

docs.title: Why?
docs.text: Look carefully.

docs.title: Thread t1
docs.text:
synchronized(lock) {
    Thread.sleep(2000);
}

Steps:

1️⃣ t1 acquires the lock 2️⃣ t1 enters synchronized block 3️⃣ t1 calls sleep(2000) 4️⃣ sleep does NOT release the lock

So t1 holds the lock for 2 seconds.

docs.title: Thread t2
docs.text:
synchronized(lock) {
    System.out.println("t2 entered");
}

But t2 needs the same lock.

Since t1 is holding the lock:

t2 → BLOCKED state

t2 must wait until t1 exits the synchronized block.

docs.title: Timeline
docs.text:
t1 start
t1 acquires lock
t1 sleeps 2 sec (still holding lock)

t2 tries to enter synchronized(lock)
t2 BLOCKED

2 seconds later:
t1 exits block
lock released

t2 enters
print "t2 entered"

docs.title: 🔥 Important Rule
docs.text:
Method Releases Lock? sleep() ❌ No wait() ✅ Yes

This is one of the most common Java interview traps.

docs.title: 🧠 Interview One-Liner
docs.text: Thread.sleep() pauses the thread but keeps the monitor lock, while wait() releases the lock and allows other threads to acquire it.

docs.title: Next Critical Concept (Core Multithreading)
docs.text: We now move to synchronized internals + monitor locks.

docs.title: 1️⃣ What is a Lock?
docs.text:
In multithreading, a lock is a mechanism that ensures:

Only one thread at a time can access a critical section of code.

Think of it like a room with one key.

Example analogy:

Room = critical code
Key = lock
Thread = person

Only the person holding the key can enter the room.

Other people must wait outside.

docs.title: 2️⃣ What is synchronized?
docs.text:
synchronized is a Java keyword used to protect critical sections.

It ensures:

Only one thread can execute that block/method at a time.

It works using monitor locks.

docs.title: A) Synchronized Block
docs.text:
You explicitly specify the lock object.

Object lock = new Object();

synchronized(lock) {
    // critical section
}

Here:

  • lock object acts as the monitor
  • Only one thread holding that lock can enter

docs.title: B) Synchronized Method
docs.text:
Example:

class Test {

    synchronized void m1() {
        // critical section
    }

}

Here Java automatically uses:

this (current object) as lock

Equivalent code:

void m1() {
    synchronized(this) {
        // critical section
    }
}

docs.title: 4️⃣ Static Synchronized Method
docs.text:
Example:

class Test {

    static synchronized void m3() {
        // critical section
    }

}

Here the lock is:

Class object

Equivalent to:

synchronized(Test.class) {
    // code
}

docs.title: 5️⃣ Summary Table
docs.text: Type Lock Used synchronized(obj) obj synchronized method this (object) static synchronized Class object

docs.title: 6️⃣ Why Locks Are Needed (Race Condition Fix)
docs.text:
Earlier problem:

count++

Two threads modify same variable → wrong result.

Solution:

synchronized void inc() {
    count++;
}

Now only one thread can execute inc() at a time.

docs.title: Now You Can Answer My Question
docs.text:
Given:

class Test {

    synchronized void m1() {}

    synchronized void m2() {}

    static synchronized void m3() {}

}

How many different locks exist here?

Hint:

  • object lock
  • class lock

✅ Answer: — two different locks exist here.

Good. Now let’s explain clearly so it becomes crystal clear for interviews.

docs.title: Code
docs.text:
class Test {

    synchronized void m1() {}

    synchronized void m2() {}

    static synchronized void m3() {}

}

docs.title: 1️⃣ Lock for m1() and m2()
docs.text:
These are instance synchronized methods.

Java internally converts them to:

void m1() {
    synchronized(this) {
    }
}

void m2() {
    synchronized(this) {
    }
}

So both methods use the same lock:

this (current object)

Meaning:

If one thread executes m1(), another thread cannot execute m2() on the same object.

Because both require the same lock.

docs.title: 2️⃣ Lock for m3()
docs.text:
This is a static synchronized method.

Java internally converts it to:

synchronized(Test.class) {
}

So the lock is:

Test.class

This is a class-level lock, not object lock.

docs.title: Final Lock Summary
docs.text:
Method Lock Used m1() object lock (this) m2() object lock (this) m3() class lock (Test.class)

So:

Total locks = 2

docs.title: 🔥 Important Interview Insight
docs.text:
Object locks and class locks are independent.

So this is possible:

Thread 1:

obj.m1();

Thread 2:

Test.m3();

Both can run at the same time.

docs.title: 🧠 Interview One-Liner
docs.text: Instance synchronized methods use the object monitor (this), while static synchronized methods use the class monitor (ClassName.class).

docs.title: Next Critical Trap
docs.text:
Predict behavior:

class Test {

    synchronized void m1() {
        System.out.println("m1 start");
        try { Thread.sleep(2000); } catch(Exception e) {}
        System.out.println("m1 end");
    }

    synchronized void m2() {
        System.out.println("m2");
    }

}

Test t = new Test();

Thread t1 = new Thread(() -> t.m1());
Thread t2 = new Thread(() -> t.m2());

t1.start();
t2.start();

Question:

Will "m2" print immediately or after 2 seconds? Why?

ANswer: ❌ Not immediately. It will print after ~2 seconds.

docs.title: Why (very important)
docs.text:
Both m1() and m2() are instance synchronized methods, so they use the same lock:

the object lock = t (i.e., this)

docs.title: What happens:
docs.text:
• t1 enters t.m1() and acquires lock on t
  • t1 sleeps 2 seconds but still holds the lock (sleep does not release lock)
  • t2 tries to enter t.m2() but cannot, because lock is still held
  • After 2 seconds, t1 exits m1() and releases lock
  • Then t2 enters m2() and prints "m2"

So "m2" prints after "m1 end" (after ~2 seconds).

docs.title: Key interview line
docs.text: Two synchronized instance methods on the same object block each other because they use the same monitor (this).

docs.title: Quick follow-up trap
docs.text:
If t2 called new Test().m2() instead of t.m2(), would it print immediately? (Yes/No)

✅ Answer: — Yes, it would print immediately.

docs.title: Why
docs.text:
Because:

  • t1 is locking on object t
  • t2 would lock on a different object new Test()

Different objects → different monitors → no blocking.

Interview one-liner:

synchronized locks are per-object. Two threads only block each other if they synchronize on the same monitor object.

docs.title: Next Level: Deadlock (Most asked trap)
docs.text:
Consider:

Object A = new Object();
Object B = new Object();

Thread t1 = new Thread(() -> {
  synchronized(A) {
    synchronized(B) {
      System.out.println("t1 done");
    }
  }
});

Thread t2 = new Thread(() -> {
  synchronized(B) {
    synchronized(A) {
      System.out.println("t2 done");
    }
  }
});

docs.title: Question:
docs.text:
1. What problem can happen here?
  2. How to fix it (one practical rule)?

docs.title: Answer:
docs.text:
Deadlock occurs when threads acquire locks in different order causing circular wait. The common fix is enforcing a consistent lock acquisition order.

Let’s go step by step.

docs.title: 1️⃣ What Problem Happens Here?
docs.text:
✔ Deadlock

Deadlock means:

Two or more threads wait forever for each other’s locks.

docs.title: How Deadlock Happens Here
docs.text:
We have two locks:

Lock A
Lock B

docs.title: Thread t1
docs.text:
Lock A
then tries Lock B

docs.title: Thread t2
docs.text:
Lock B
then tries Lock A

docs.title: Timeline
docs.text:
t1 acquires A
t2 acquires B

t1 tries B → blocked
t2 tries A → blocked

Now:

t1 waiting for B
t2 waiting for A

Both wait forever.

This is deadlock.

docs.title: 2️⃣ Why wait()/notify() is NOT the Fix
docs.text:
wait() / notify() are used for:

Thread communication

Example:

  • Producer-consumer
  • Task coordination

They do not solve deadlocks caused by lock ordering.

docs.title: 3️⃣ Correct Fix (Most Important Rule)
docs.text:
Always acquire locks in the same order.

Example fix:

Thread t1 = new Thread(() -> {
  synchronized(A) {
    synchronized(B) {
      System.out.println("t1 done");
    }
  }
});

Thread t2 = new Thread(() -> {
  synchronized(A) {   // same order
    synchronized(B) {
      System.out.println("t2 done");
    }
  }
});

Now:

Both threads acquire A first
Then B

No circular wait → no deadlock.

docs.title: 🔥 Four Conditions for Deadlock (Interview Gold)
docs.text:
Deadlock happens when these 4 exist:

1️⃣ Mutual exclusion 2️⃣ Hold and wait 3️⃣ No preemption 4️⃣ Circular wait

Break any one → deadlock avoided.

docs.title: 🧠 Interview One-Liner
docs.text: Deadlock occurs when threads acquire locks in different order causing circular wait. The common fix is enforcing a consistent lock acquisition order.

docs.title: Next Critical Concept (Very Important)
docs.text: We now go to:

docs.title: volatile (Most misunderstood Java keyword)
docs.text: Before I explain it, tell me:

docs.title: Question
docs.text: What problem does volatile solve?

docs.title: Answer:
docs.text:
volatile ensures visibility of variable updates across threads but does not guarantee atomicity.

Let’s explain from the ground up.

docs.title: 1️⃣ The Real Problem volatile Solves
docs.text:
In multithreading there are two major problems:

1️⃣ Visibility problem 2️⃣ Atomicity problem

volatile solves only the visibility problem.

docs.title: 2️⃣ Why Visibility Problem Happens
docs.text:
Each thread has its own working memory (CPU cache).

Architecture simplified:

Main Memory (Heap)
      |
  ------------
  |          |
Thread 1   Thread 2
Cache      Cache

Threads do not always read directly from main memory.

They may read from their local cache.

docs.title: Example Problem
docs.text:
class Test {

    static boolean running = true;

    public static void main(String[] args) {

        Thread t = new Thread(() -> {
            while(running) {
            }
            System.out.println("Stopped");
        });

        t.start();

        try { Thread.sleep(2000); } catch(Exception e){}

        running = false;
    }
}

docs.title: Expected behavior
docs.text:
After 2 seconds:

Stopped

docs.title: What may happen
docs.text:
Thread may run forever.

Why?

Thread t may cache:

running = true

It keeps reading cached value, not updated value from main memory.

So it never sees:

running = false

docs.title: 3️⃣ Solution: volatile
docs.text:
static volatile boolean running = true;

Now Java guarantees:

Read directly from main memory
Write directly to main memory

No caching issue.

docs.title: 4️⃣ What volatile Guarantees
docs.text: volatile provides:

docs.title: ✔ Visibility
docs.text: Changes made by one thread are immediately visible to other threads.

docs.title: ✔ Happens-before relationship
docs.text: Write → immediately visible to readers.

docs.title: 5️⃣ What volatile DOES NOT Solve
docs.text:
volatile does NOT make operations atomic.

Example:

volatile int count = 0;

count++;

Still unsafe.

Because count++ =

read
add
write

Multiple threads can break it.

So for increments we need:

synchronized
AtomicInteger
Lock

docs.title: 6️⃣ Interview Table
docs.text: Feature volatile Visibility ✔ Atomicity ❌ Prevent caching ✔ Locking ❌

docs.title: 🧠 Interview One-Liner
docs.text: volatile ensures visibility of variable updates across threads but does not guarantee atomicity.

docs.title: Now a Very Important Trap
docs.text:
Predict output:

class Test {

    volatile int count = 0;

    void inc() {
        count++;
    }

}

If 10 threads run inc() 1000 times each:

Expected:

10000

Will we always get 10000?

Yes or No — and why?

docs.title: Answer:
docs.text:
volatile ensures visibility but does not guarantee atomicity, so operations like count++ remain unsafe in multithreading.

Now let’s explanation to interview-quality clarity.

docs.title: Correct Answer
docs.text: ❌ No, we will NOT always get 10000.

docs.title: Why?
docs.text:
Because:

count++

is not atomic.

Even though count is volatile, the operation still executes as three steps:

1. Read count
2. Add 1
3. Write count

Example race condition:

Initial:

count = 5

Thread 1 reads:

5

Thread 2 reads:

5

Both increment:

6

Both write:

6

One increment is lost.

docs.title: What volatile Actually Guarantees
docs.text:
✔ Visibility ✔ Prevents CPU caching issues

But it does NOT guarantee:

❌ Atomic operations ❌ Mutual exclusion

docs.title: Option 1 — synchronized
docs.text:
synchronized void inc() {
    count++;
}

docs.title: Option 2 — AtomicInteger (best)
docs.text:
AtomicInteger count = new AtomicInteger();

count.incrementAndGet();

Uses CAS (Compare-And-Swap).

docs.title: Interview One-Liner
docs.text: volatile ensures visibility but does not guarantee atomicity, so operations like count++ remain unsafe in multithreading.

docs.title: Now a Very Important Multithreading Trap
docs.text:
Predict the output:

class Test {

    volatile boolean flag = false;

    void writer() {
        flag = true;
    }

    void reader() {
        if(flag) {
            System.out.println("Visible");
        }
    }

}

Two threads run:

Thread 1 → writer()
Thread 2 → reader()

Question:

Is it guaranteed that thread 2 sees flag = true?

Yes / No — and why?

docs.title: volatile ensures visibility of updates across threads, but it does not guarantee ordering of thread execution.
Correct Answer
docs.text:
✔ Yes — if thread 2 reads flag after thread 1 writes it, it will see true.

But we must add an important condition.

docs.title: Why?
docs.text:
Because flag is declared as:

volatile boolean flag = false;

volatile guarantees visibility between threads.

Meaning:

Write by one thread → immediately visible to other threads

So when thread 1 does:

flag = true;

the updated value is written to main memory.

When thread 2 reads:

if(flag)

it must read from main memory, not a cached copy.

docs.title: Important Detail (Interview Trap)
docs.text:
volatile does NOT guarantee execution order between threads.

So two scenarios exist:

docs.title: Scenario 1
docs.text:
Thread 1 executes first.

writer() → flag = true
reader() → sees true

Output:

Visible

docs.title: Scenario 2
docs.text:
Thread 2 executes first.

reader() → flag still false
writer() → later sets true

Output:

(no output)

docs.title: Therefore the correct statement
docs.text: volatile guarantees visibility, but not thread execution order.

docs.title: Interview One-Liner
docs.text: volatile ensures visibility of updates across threads, but it does not guarantee ordering of thread execution.

docs.title: Next Critical Topic (Very Important)
docs.text:
Now we move to Atomic Variables & CAS (Compare-And-Swap) — this is used inside:

  • AtomicInteger
  • ConcurrentHashMap
  • many lock-free algorithms.

First question for you:

docs.title: Question
docs.text: Why is AtomicInteger faster than synchronized in many cases?

docs.title: Answer:
docs.text:
AtomicInteger uses lock-free CAS operations at the CPU level, avoiding blocking and context switching, which makes it faster than synchronized in many scenarios.

Now let’s explain the real reason deeply, because this is an important interview topic.

docs.title: Why AtomicInteger is Often Faster than synchronized
docs.text:
Your intuition is correct: it avoids heavy locking.

The main reason is:

AtomicInteger uses CAS (Compare-And-Swap) instead of locks.

docs.title: 1️⃣ How synchronized Works
docs.text:
When a thread enters:

synchronized void inc() {
    count++;
}

The JVM must:

1️⃣ Acquire monitor lock 2️⃣ Block other threads 3️⃣ Execute code 4️⃣ Release lock

If many threads compete:

Thread waiting
Context switching
Lock management

These operations are expensive.

docs.title: 2️⃣ How AtomicInteger Works
docs.text:
AtomicInteger uses a lock-free algorithm.

Example:

AtomicInteger count = new AtomicInteger();

count.incrementAndGet();

Internally it uses CAS (Compare-And-Swap).

docs.title: 3️⃣ CAS (Compare-And-Swap)
docs.text:
CAS is a CPU-level atomic instruction.

It works like this:

compare current value with expected value
if equal → update
if not equal → retry

Example:

Initial:

count = 5

Thread wants to update:

expected = 5
newValue = 6

CPU instruction:

if(count == 5)
   count = 6
else
   retry

This happens without locking.

docs.title: 4️⃣ Why CAS is Faster
docs.text:
CAS:

✔ No thread blocking ✔ No OS context switching ✔ CPU-level atomic operation

So performance is usually better under moderate contention.

docs.title: Interview Comparison
docs.text: Feature synchronized AtomicInteger Locking Yes No Blocking Yes No Uses CAS No Yes Performance Slower under contention Usually faster

docs.title: Interview One-Liner
docs.text: AtomicInteger uses lock-free CAS operations at the CPU level, avoiding blocking and context switching, which makes it faster than synchronized in many scenarios.

docs.title: Important Limitation
docs.text:
CAS can suffer from a problem called:

ABA problem

We’ll discuss that later.

docs.title: Next Important Trap
docs.text:
Predict the behavior:

AtomicInteger count = new AtomicInteger(0);

Thread t1 = new Thread(() -> {
    for(int i=0;i<1000;i++)
        count.incrementAndGet();
});

Thread t2 = new Thread(() -> {
    for(int i=0;i<1000;i++)
        count.incrementAndGet();
});

t1.start();
t2.start();
t1.join();
t2.join();

System.out.println(count);

docs.title: Question:
docs.text: Will this always print 2000? Why?

docs.title: Answer:
docs.text:
Yes — it will always print 2000.
AtomicInteger uses CAS (Compare-And-Swap) in a retry loop to ensure atomic updates without using locks.

docs.title: Correct Answer
docs.text: ✔ Yes — it will always print 2000.

docs.title: Why?
docs.text:
Because:

count.incrementAndGet();

is an atomic operation.

Internally it uses CAS (Compare-And-Swap).

docs.title: Internal Working (Simplified)
docs.text:
Suppose:

count = 5

Thread tries to update:

expected = 5
newValue = 6

CPU executes CAS:

if(count == expected)
    count = newValue
else
    retry

If another thread changed the value meanwhile:

CAS fails → retry loop

So no updates are lost.

docs.title: Internal Loop (conceptually)
docs.text:
AtomicInteger works like this:

while(true) {
   int current = value;
   int next = current + 1;

   if(CAS(current, next))
       return next;
}

CAS success → operation done CAS failure → retry

docs.title: Important Interview Point
docs.text:
AtomicInteger guarantees:

✔ Atomicity ✔ Visibility ✔ Lock-free behavior

docs.title: Interview One-Liner
docs.text: AtomicInteger uses CAS (Compare-And-Swap) in a retry loop to ensure atomic updates without using locks.

docs.title: Now a Very Important Multithreading Trap
docs.text:
Predict output possibility:

class Test {

    int x = 0;
    int y = 0;

    void writer() {
        x = 1;
        y = 1;
    }

    void reader() {
        if(y == 1) {
            System.out.println(x);
        }
    }

}

Two threads:

Thread 1 → writer()
Thread 2 → reader()

docs.title: Question:
docs.text:
Is it possible for this program to print:

0

Yes or No — and why?

This question leads to Java Memory Model & instruction reordering, one of the most important multithreading topics.

docs.title: Answer:
docs.text:
Yes — it is possible to print 0 (in some executions), because without volatile/synchronization there is no happens-before relationship between the threads.

Let me explain clearly.

docs.title: Why 0 is possible
docs.text:
You’re thinking in single-thread order:

writer sets x=1 then y=1, so if reader sees y==1 it must see x==1.

That guarantee exists only in single-thread reasoning.

In multithreading, without proper memory barriers, two things can happen:

docs.title: 1) Visibility issue
docs.text:
Thread 1 updates x and y, but thread 2 may see:

  • the update to y
  • but still see old cached value of x

So reader can observe:

y == 1
x == 0

docs.title: 2) Reordering
docs.text:
The compiler/CPU can reorder writes for performance (as long as single-thread behavior stays correct). So it’s possible that:

  • y=1 becomes visible before x=1

Again reader can see y==1 first.

docs.title: Key concept: Happens-Before
docs.text:
Because there is no synchronization:

  • writer’s actions are not guaranteed to be visible in same order to reader.

So 0 is possible.

docs.title: How to Fix It (two correct ways)
Option A: Make y volatile
docs.text:
volatile int y = 0;

If writer does:

x = 1;
y = 1;

And reader checks:

if (y == 1) System.out.println(x);

Now volatile write/read creates a happens-before:

  • If reader sees y==1, it will also see x==1.

docs.title: Option B: Use synchronized
docs.text: Put writer and reader under same lock.

docs.title: Interview one-liner
docs.text: Without a happens-before relation, reads can see stale values and writes can appear reordered, so y==1 does not guarantee x==1.

docs.title: Quick check question (yes/no)
docs.text:
If we make only x volatile (not y), is printing 0 still possible?

Let’s go step-by-step with a mental model.

We’ll answer both questions:

  1. Why volatile int y fixes the issue
  2. When this problem happens (same object vs different object)

docs.title: 1️⃣ First Understand the Problem Clearly
docs.text:
Code:

class Test {

    int x = 0;
    int y = 0;

    void writer() {
        x = 1;
        y = 1;
    }

    void reader() {
        if (y == 1) {
            System.out.println(x);
        }
    }
}

Two threads:

Thread 1 → writer()
Thread 2 → reader()

You assumed:

x = 1
y = 1

So if y == 1, then x must be 1.

But multithreading breaks that assumption.

docs.title: 2️⃣ Why 0 Can Print
docs.text:
Modern CPUs and JVM optimize execution.

Two things may happen:

docs.title: A) CPU Cache (Visibility Problem)
docs.text:
Each thread may read from its own CPU cache, not main memory.

Example:

Main memory:

x = 1
y = 1

Thread 2 cache may still have:

x = 0
y = 1

So it prints:

0

docs.title: B) Instruction Reordering
docs.text:
The JVM or CPU may reorder instructions.

Writer code:

x = 1
y = 1

May internally execute like:

y = 1
x = 1

Then reader may see:

y == 1
x == 0

Again prints:

0

docs.title: 3️⃣ How volatile Fixes It
docs.text:
If we declare:

volatile int y = 0;

Then y becomes a memory barrier.

Writer:

x = 1;
y = 1;   // volatile write

Reader:

if (y == 1) {  // volatile read
    System.out.println(x);
}

Now JVM guarantees:

All writes before volatile write
must be visible to threads reading that volatile variable

Meaning:

x = 1 happens-before y = 1

So if reader sees:

y == 1

then it must see:

x == 1

Now 0 cannot happen.

docs.title: Interview One-Liner
docs.text: A volatile write establishes a happens-before relationship with subsequent volatile reads, ensuring visibility of prior writes.

docs.title: 4️⃣ Your Second Question (Very Important)
docs.text:
You asked:

Does this happen when two threads operate on the same object or different objects?

docs.title: Answer
docs.text:
The problem occurs when:

Two threads access shared variables

It does NOT depend on object count, but on shared memory.

Example:

docs.title: Case A — Same Object
docs.text:
Test obj = new Test();
Thread 1 → obj.writer()
Thread 2 → obj.reader()

Shared variables:

obj.x
obj.y

Problem possible.

docs.title: Case B — Static Variables
docs.text:
static int x
static int y

All objects share them → problem possible.

docs.title: Case C — Separate Objects
docs.text:
Test obj1 = new Test();
Test obj2 = new Test();

Thread 1 uses obj1 Thread 2 uses obj2

Now variables are not shared → no issue.

docs.title: Rule to Remember
docs.text:
Multithreading problems occur when:

Shared mutable state + multiple threads

docs.title: 5️⃣ Visual Memory Model
docs.text:
Main Memory
   x=1 y=1
     |
---------------------
|                   |
Thread 1 Cache   Thread 2 Cache
 x=1 y=1         x=0 y=1

Reader prints:

0

docs.title: Quick Check (Important)
docs.text:
If we declare:

volatile int x;
volatile int y;

docs.title: Will the problem still happen?
docs.text: Yes or No — and why?

docs.title: Answer:
docs.text:
No

Answer “No” is correct, but let’s understand why, because this touches the Java Memory Model (JMM) and the concept of happens-before.

docs.title: If both variables are volatile
docs.text:
volatile int x = 0;
volatile int y = 0;

void writer() {
    x = 1;
    y = 1;
}

void reader() {
    if (y == 1) {
        System.out.println(x);
    }
}

Two threads:

Thread 1 → writer()
Thread 2 → reader()

docs.title: What volatile guarantees
docs.text:
A volatile write has two important guarantees:

1️⃣ Visibility The value written by one thread becomes immediately visible to other threads.

2️⃣ Ordering (memory barrier) Operations before a volatile write cannot be reordered after it.

docs.title: Execution Order With Volatile
docs.text:
Writer thread executes:

x = 1
y = 1   (volatile write)

Because y is volatile:

x = 1  happens-before  y = 1

docs.title: Reader Thread
docs.text:
Reader does:

read y (volatile read)

A volatile read guarantees that the thread sees all writes that happened before the volatile write.

So if the reader sees:

y == 1

Then the reader must see:

x == 1

docs.title: Therefore
docs.text:
Printing:

0

is not possible when y is volatile (or both are volatile).

docs.title: Important Interview Rule
docs.text:
A volatile write establishes a happens-before relationship with subsequent reads of that variable.

So:

write volatile → read volatile

ensures memory visibility.

docs.title: Important Detail
docs.text:
Actually only y needs to be volatile in this example. x does not have to be volatile.

Because the volatile access to y acts as the memory synchronization point.

docs.title: Interview One-Liner
docs.text: A volatile write creates a happens-before relationship with subsequent volatile reads, ensuring that all previous writes become visible to other threads.

docs.title: Quick Challenge (Very Important Interview Trap)
docs.text:
Predict if this program can print 0.

class Test {

    volatile boolean ready = false;
    int number = 0;

    void writer() {
        number = 42;
        ready = true;
    }

    void reader() {
        if (ready) {
            System.out.println(number);
        }
    }
}

Two threads run writer() and reader().

Question: Can it print 0 or will it always print 42? Why?

Not this time — ✅ with ready as volatile, it should not print 0 if the reader sees ready == true.

Here’s the exact rule:

docs.title: What volatile ready guarantees
docs.text:
In writer():

number = 42;     // normal write
ready = true;    // volatile write

A volatile write (ready = true) creates a happens-before edge:

All writes before the volatile write become visible to any thread that later reads that volatile variable as true.

So in reader():

if (ready) {          // volatile read
    System.out.println(number);
}

If the reader sees ready == true, then it must see number == 42.

docs.title: What outputs are possible?
docs.text:
• If reader runs before writer sets ready=true: it prints nothing.
  • If reader runs after it sees ready=true: it prints 42.

✅ Printing 0 is not allowed in a correctly implemented JVM under the Java Memory Model, given ready is volatile.

docs.title: Interview one-liner
docs.text: Volatile is often used as a “publish” flag: writing the flag volatile safely publishes prior writes.

docs.title: Quick check (trap)
docs.text: If we remove volatile from ready, is printing 0 possible? (Yes/No)

docs.title: Answer:
docs.text:
Yes, printing 0 becomes possible if ready is not volatile.

Let’s understand why, because this is a very important Java Memory Model concept.

docs.title: Code Without volatile
docs.text:
class Test {

    boolean ready = false;
    int number = 0;

    void writer() {
        number = 42;
        ready = true;
    }

    void reader() {
        if (ready) {
            System.out.println(number);
        }
    }
}

Two threads:

Thread 1 → writer()
Thread 2 → reader()

docs.title: Why 0 Can Print
docs.text:
Without volatile, there is no happens-before relationship between the threads.

Two issues may occur:

docs.title: 1️⃣ Visibility Problem
docs.text:
Thread 1 updates memory:

number = 42
ready = true

But Thread 2 might see:

ready = true
number = 0

Because it reads stale cached values.

So output:

0

docs.title: 2️⃣ Instruction Reordering
docs.text:
The CPU or JVM may reorder instructions.

Writer code:

number = 42
ready = true

May execute internally as:

ready = true
number = 42

Now the reader sees:

ready == true
number == 0

Again prints:

0

docs.title: Why volatile ready Fixes It
docs.text:
When ready is volatile:

number = 42
ready = true   (volatile write)

Reader:

if(ready)   (volatile read)

Java guarantees:

All writes before volatile write
are visible after volatile read

So if reader sees:

ready == true

it must see:

number == 42

docs.title: Visual Timeline
docs.text:
Without volatile:

Writer Thread          Reader Thread

number = 42
ready = true  ------->  ready == true
                        number == 0

Possible output:

0

docs.title: Interview One-Liner
docs.text: Without synchronization or volatile, there is no happens-before relationship, so a thread may observe writes out of order or see stale values.

docs.title: Thread Pools & Executors
docs.text:
We should now move to Thread Pools & Executors.

Because in real backend systems:

Threads are rarely created manually.

They are managed by:

ExecutorService
ThreadPoolExecutor
ForkJoinPool

Before we start that, one quick check:

docs.title: Question
docs.text:
Why is creating too many threads dangerous?

Give two reasons.

docs.title: Answer:
docs.text: Creating too many threads increases memory usage, context switching overhead, and scheduler pressure, which can severely degrade performance.

docs.title: Why Creating Too Many Threads is Dangerous
1️⃣ Memory Consumption (Very Important)
docs.text:
Each thread has its own stack memory.

Typical stack size:

512 KB – 1 MB per thread

If you create:

10,000 threads

Memory usage:

~10 GB stack memory

Your JVM can crash with:

OutOfMemoryError: unable to create new native thread

docs.title: 2️⃣ Context Switching Overhead
docs.text:
CPU can run only a few threads at a time.

If you create too many threads:

CPU keeps switching between threads

This is called:

Context Switching

Switching threads costs CPU time.

So performance actually becomes worse, not better.

docs.title: 3️⃣ Thread Scheduling Overhead
docs.text:
The OS scheduler must manage all threads:

ready queue
priority
scheduling

More threads → more overhead.

docs.title: 4️⃣ Resource Contention
docs.text:
Threads compete for:

CPU
memory
locks
I/O

Too many threads increases lock contention and reduces throughput.

docs.title: Real Solution: Thread Pools
docs.text:
Instead of creating many threads:

Create limited threads
Reuse them

Example:

ExecutorService pool = Executors.newFixedThreadPool(10);

Now only 10 threads execute tasks.

docs.title: Interview One-Liner
docs.text: Creating too many threads increases memory usage, context switching overhead, and scheduler pressure, which can severely degrade performance.

docs.title: Now We Enter a Very Important Topic
Thread Pool & Executor Framework
docs.text: Before I explain the internals, answer this:

docs.title: Question
docs.text:
What is the difference between:

execute()

and

submit()

in ExecutorService?

docs.title: Answer:
docs.text:
execute() runs a task without returning a result, while submit() returns a Future that allows retrieving results and exceptions.

Let’s build it step by step.

docs.title: 1️⃣ What is ExecutorService?
docs.text:
Instead of creating threads like this:

new Thread(task).start();

Java recommends using a thread pool:

ExecutorService pool = Executors.newFixedThreadPool(5);

Now:

  • Only 5 threads exist
  • Tasks are queued
  • Threads reuse themselves

Example:

pool.execute(() -> {
    System.out.println(Thread.currentThread().getName());
});

docs.title: 2️⃣ execute() vs submit()
docs.text: Both are used to send tasks to a thread pool, but they behave differently.

docs.title: execute()
docs.text:
Method signature:

void execute(Runnable command)

Example:

pool.execute(() -> {
    System.out.println("Task running");
});

Characteristics:

  • Takes Runnable only
  • No return value
  • Cannot capture result
  • Cannot track completion easily

docs.title: submit()
docs.text:
Method signature:

Future<?> submit(Runnable task)
Future<T> submit(Callable<T> task)

Example:

Future<Integer> result = pool.submit(() -> {
    return 10 + 20;
});

Get result:

System.out.println(result.get());

Output:

30

docs.title: Key Difference Table
docs.text: Feature execute() submit() Return value ❌ None ✅ Future Accepts Callable ❌ No ✅ Yes Get result ❌ No ✅ Yes Exception handling Goes to thread's handler Stored in Future

docs.title: Important Interview Insight
docs.text: If a task throws exception:

docs.title: execute()
docs.text: Exception goes to thread's uncaught exception handler.

docs.title: submit()
docs.text:
Exception is stored inside Future.

You see it only when calling:

future.get();

docs.title: Interview One-Liner
docs.text: execute() runs a task without returning a result, while submit() returns a Future that allows retrieving results and exceptions.

docs.title: 3️⃣ What is Future?
docs.text:
Future represents result of asynchronous computation.

Methods:

future.get()       // wait for result
future.isDone()    // task finished?
future.cancel()    // cancel task

docs.title: 4️⃣ Runnable vs Callable
docs.text:
Runnable:

void run()

Callable:

T call()

Callable can:

✔ return result ✔ throw checked exceptions

docs.title: Quick Check Question
docs.text:
What will happen here?

ExecutorService pool = Executors.newFixedThreadPool(2);

Future<Integer> f = pool.submit(() -> {
    int x = 10 / 0;
    return x;
});

System.out.println("Done");

Question:

Will the program crash immediately or continue printing "Done"?

Then what happens when we call:

f.get();

Answer:

  • “after f.get() it will get divide by zero exception” → “when f.get() is called, it will throw the exception.”

Now let’s explain what exactly happens internally (important for interviews).

docs.title: Code
docs.text:
ExecutorService pool = Executors.newFixedThreadPool(2);

Future<Integer> f = pool.submit(() -> {
    int x = 10 / 0;
    return x;
});

System.out.println("Done");

docs.title: 1️⃣ Task submitted to thread pool
docs.text:
submit() does not execute immediately in the main thread.

Instead:

Main thread → submit task → task goes to queue
Thread pool worker → executes task

docs.title: 2️⃣ Main thread continues
docs.text:
Main thread prints:

Done

because the task runs asynchronously.

So program does not crash here.

docs.title: 3️⃣ Worker thread executes task
docs.text:
Inside thread pool worker:

int x = 10 / 0;

Exception occurs:

ArithmeticException: / by zero

But because task was submitted using submit():

Exception is captured inside Future

It is not thrown immediately.

docs.title: 4️⃣ When f.get() is called
docs.text:
Example:

f.get();

Now Java unwraps the stored exception and throws:

ExecutionException

Inside it:

ArithmeticException: / by zero

docs.title: Important Interview Detail
docs.text:
submit() wraps exceptions in:

java.util.concurrent.ExecutionException

So actual exception is inside:

e.getCause()

Example:

try {
    f.get();
} catch (ExecutionException e) {
    System.out.println(e.getCause());
}

docs.title: Key Difference With execute()
docs.text:
If we used:

pool.execute(() -> {
    int x = 10 / 0;
});

Then:

ArithmeticException

would go to UncaughtExceptionHandler immediately.

docs.title: Interview One-Liner
docs.text: When using submit(), exceptions are captured inside the Future and thrown as ExecutionException when Future.get() is called.

docs.title: Next Important Multithreading Concept
docs.text:
Now we move to ThreadPoolExecutor Internals — this is where senior interview questions come from.

First question:

docs.title: Why does a ThreadPool need a task queue?
docs.text: What problem does the queue solve?

docs.title: Answer:
docs.text:
