docs.text: Even if a return statement exists in the try block, the finally block executes before the method returns.

docs.title: Super Famous Java Interview Trap
docs.text:
What happens here?

public static int test() {

    try {
        return 10;
    }
    finally {
        return 20;
    }

}

What will this method return?

docs.title: Answer:
docs.text: ✅ — the method returns 20.

docs.title: Why
docs.text:
Code:

public static int test() {
    try {
        return 10;
    } finally {
        return 20;
    }
}

Execution flow:

  1. try executes → return 10 is prepared (value saved).
  2. Before the method actually returns, the finally block runs.
  3. finally has its own return, which overrides the previous return.

So the final result becomes:

20

docs.title: Key Rule
docs.text: A return in finally overrides any return from try or catch.

docs.title: Important Interview Note ⚠
docs.text:
This is considered bad practice, because it can:

  • hide exceptions
  • override earlier return values
  • make debugging difficult

Example problem:

try {
    throw new RuntimeException();
} finally {
    return 10;   // hides the exception!
}

The exception is lost.

✅ Interview one-liner

A return statement inside finally overrides any previous return from try or catch, which is why using return in finally is discouraged.

If you're okay, the next very important exception topic (often asked in interviews) is:

try-with-resources and suppressed exceptions.

It explains how Java automatically closes resources like files, streams, and database connections.

docs.title: try-with-resources
docs.text:
Java introduced try-with-resources in Java 7 to automatically close resources like:

File
Stream
Socket
Database Connection
Scanner

This prevents resource leaks.

docs.title: 1️⃣ Problem Before Java 7
docs.text:
Old code:

FileInputStream file = null;

try {
    file = new FileInputStream("data.txt");
}
catch(IOException e) {
}
finally {
    if(file != null) {
        file.close();
    }
}

Problems:

verbose code
easy to forget closing
resource leak risk

docs.title: 2️⃣ try-with-resources Solution
docs.text:
Java allows declaring resources inside try().

Example:

try(FileInputStream file =
        new FileInputStream("data.txt")) {

    // use file

}

JVM automatically calls:

file.close()

after the block finishes.

docs.title: 3️⃣ How It Works Internally
docs.text:
The resource must implement:

AutoCloseable

or

Closeable

Example hierarchy:

AutoCloseable
      ↑
   Closeable
      ↑
FileInputStream
BufferedReader
Scanner

Because these implement close().

docs.title: 4️⃣ Example
docs.text:
try(Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in)) {

    int x = sc.nextInt();
    System.out.println(x);

}

After execution:

sc.close()

is called automatically.

docs.title: 5️⃣ Multiple Resources
docs.text:
You can declare multiple resources.

try(
    FileInputStream f = new FileInputStream("a.txt");
    BufferedReader br = new BufferedReader(new InputStreamReader(f))
) {

}

Close order:

br.close()
f.close()

Reverse order.

docs.title: Suppressed Exceptions
docs.text:
Suppose this happens:

Exception in try block
Exception in close()

Example:

try(Resource r = new Resource()) {
    throw new RuntimeException("try error");
}

and

close() throws another exception

Now Java must decide which exception to show.

Rule:

Primary exception → try block exception
Suppressed exception → close() exception

Java keeps suppressed exceptions inside the main exception.

Example output:

Exception in try
Suppressed: exception in close()

docs.title: 7️⃣ Access Suppressed Exceptions
docs.text: e.getSuppressed();

docs.title: Interview One-liner
docs.text: try-with-resources automatically closes resources that implement AutoCloseable, preventing resource leaks.

docs.title: Very Common Interview Question
docs.text:
Which interface must a resource implement to be used in try-with-resources?

A) Serializable
B) AutoCloseable
C) Runnable
D) Cloneable

What do you think?

docs.title: Answer:
docs.text: “AutoClosable interface resource mush implement to try with resources so auto close the resource.” → “A resource must implement the AutoCloseable interface to be used in try-with-resources so that it can be automatically closed.”

docs.title: Correct Answer
docs.text: B) AutoCloseable

docs.title: Why AutoCloseable?
docs.text:
Any resource used in:

try (resource) { }

must implement:

AutoCloseable

Because Java will automatically call:

close()

when the block finishes.

docs.title: AutoCloseable Interface
docs.text:
Definition:

public interface AutoCloseable {
    void close() throws Exception;
}

So when the try block ends:

resource.close()

is executed automatically.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
try(Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in)) {
    int x = sc.nextInt();
}

After execution:

sc.close()

runs automatically.

docs.title: Many Java Classes Implement AutoCloseable
docs.text:
Examples:

Scanner
FileInputStream
BufferedReader
Connection (JDBC)
Statement
ResultSet
Socket

This is why try-with-resources is used heavily in database and file operations.

docs.title: Example (Database)
docs.text:
try(
    Connection con = DriverManager.getConnection(url);
    Statement st = con.createStatement();
    ResultSet rs = st.executeQuery("SELECT * FROM user")
) {

}

All of these will automatically close:

rs.close()
st.close()
con.close()

docs.title: Interview One-Liner
docs.text: A resource must implement AutoCloseable to be used in try-with-resources, allowing Java to automatically call close() when the block finishes.

docs.title: Vishwambhar — Important
docs.text:
You have now covered almost the entire Exception Handling topic:

Exception hierarchy
Checked vs Unchecked
try / catch / finally
throw vs throws
Exception propagation
Multiple catch blocks
try-with-resources
Suppressed exceptions

This is strong interview preparation.

docs.title: Next Small But Important Topic
docs.text: One more concept remains in exceptions:

docs.title: Custom Exceptions
docs.text:
Example:

if(age < 18) {
    throw new InvalidAgeException();
}

Before I explain:

docs.title: Quick question
docs.text:
Custom exceptions should usually extend:

A) Throwable
B) Exception
C) Error
D) Object

What do you think?

docs.title: Answer:
docs.text:
✅ — B) Exception is the typical choice.

“Custom exceptions usually extend Exception when we want a checked exception, or RuntimeException when we want an unchecked exception.”

docs.title: Custom Exceptions in Java
docs.text:
Custom exceptions allow you to create application-specific errors.

Example cases:

InvalidAgeException
InsufficientBalanceException
UserNotFoundException
OrderLimitExceededException

docs.title: 1️⃣ Checked Custom Exception
docs.text:
Extend:

Exception

Example:

class InvalidAgeException extends Exception {

    public InvalidAgeException(String message) {
        super(message);
    }

}

Usage:

public void vote(int age) throws InvalidAgeException {

    if(age < 18) {
        throw new InvalidAgeException("Age must be >= 18");
    }

}

Compiler forces handling.

docs.title: 2️⃣ Unchecked Custom Exception
docs.text:
Extend:

RuntimeException

Example:

class InvalidAgeException extends RuntimeException {

    public InvalidAgeException(String message) {
        super(message);
    }

}

Now compiler does not force handling.

docs.title: When to Use Which?
docs.text: Type Use Case Checked Exception recoverable conditions RuntimeException programming errors

docs.title: Real Example (Bank System)
docs.text:
class InsufficientBalanceException extends Exception {
    public InsufficientBalanceException(String msg){
        super(msg);
    }
}

Usage:

if(balance < withdrawAmount) {
    throw new InsufficientBalanceException("Balance too low");
}

docs.title: Best Practice for Custom Exceptions
docs.text:
Always include:

constructor with message
constructor with cause

Example:

public class MyException extends Exception {

    public MyException(String message) {
        super(message);
    }

    public MyException(String message, Throwable cause) {
        super(message, cause);
    }

}

docs.title: Interview One-liner
docs.text:
Custom exceptions are user-defined exception classes that extend Exception or RuntimeException to represent application-specific errors.

Before we move forward, one last classic interview trap:

What happens here?

try {
    throw new Exception("Error");
}
catch(RuntimeException e) {
    System.out.println("Runtime");
}
catch(Exception e) {
    System.out.println("Exception");
}

What will be printed?

docs.title: Answer:
docs.text: It will print Exception

docs.title: Step-by-Step Execution
docs.text:
Code:

try {
    throw new Exception("Error");
}
catch(RuntimeException e) {
    System.out.println("Runtime");
}
catch(Exception e) {
    System.out.println("Exception");
}

docs.title: Step 1
docs.text:
Exception thrown:

Exception

Type:

java.lang.Exception

docs.title: Step 2
docs.text:
JVM checks catch blocks from top to bottom.

First catch:

catch(RuntimeException e)

But:

Exception ≠ RuntimeException

So this block does not match.

docs.title: Step 3
docs.text:
Next catch:

catch(Exception e)

This matches because:

Exception → same type

So it executes.

Output:

Exception

docs.title: Important Interview Rule
docs.text:
Catch blocks are checked:

top → bottom
first matching catch executes

docs.title: Exception Hierarchy Here
docs.text:
Throwable
   |
   +-- Exception
          |
          +-- RuntimeException

Since RuntimeException is a subclass, it cannot catch a parent exception.

docs.title: Interview One-Liner
docs.text: The JVM checks catch blocks sequentially from top to bottom and executes the first matching exception handler.

docs.title: Exception Handling Topic Complete 🎯
docs.text:
Exception hierarchy
Checked vs Unchecked
try/catch/finally
throw vs throws
Propagation
Multiple catch
finally behavior
try-with-resources
Suppressed exceptions
Custom exceptions

docs.title: 1️⃣ Basic Definition (Interview Style)
docs.text:
Type conversion is the process of converting one data type into another. In Java, it happens in two ways:

  • Implicit (Widening)
  • Explicit (Narrowing / Casting)

docs.title: 2️⃣ Implicit Type Conversion (Widening)
docs.text:
Also called: Automatic Casting

When:

  • Smaller type → Larger type
  • No data loss

Example:

int x = 10;
long y = x;   // automatic

docs.title: Why allowed?
docs.text:
Because:

  • long (8 bytes) can hold int (4 bytes)
  • No information lost

docs.title: Primitive Widening Order (Very Important)
docs.text:
byte → short → int → long → float → double
           char ↗

⚠ boolean cannot be converted to any other type.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
byte b = 10;
int i = b;      // OK
double d = i;   // OK

docs.title: 3️⃣ Explicit Type Conversion (Narrowing)
docs.text:
When:

  • Larger type → Smaller type
  • Possible data loss
  • Must use casting operator

Example:

double d = 10.5;
int x = (int) d;

Output:

10

Decimal part removed (not rounded).

docs.title: ⚠️ Data Loss Example
docs.text:
int x = 130;
byte b = (byte) x;
System.out.println(b);

Output:

-126

Why?

Because:

  • byte range: -128 to 127
  • 130 exceeds range
  • Overflow happens
  • Binary truncation

This is very common interview trap.

docs.title: 🔥 Trap 1: Arithmetic Promotion
docs.text:
byte a = 10;
byte b = 20;
byte c = a + b;  // ERROR

Why error?

Because:

  • a + b automatically promoted to int
  • So result is int
  • Cannot assign int to byte without casting

Correct:

byte c = (byte)(a + b);

docs.title: 🔥 Trap 2: Char + Int
docs.text:
char c = 'A';
int x = c;   // OK

Output:

65

Because char stores Unicode value.

docs.title: 🔥 Trap 3: Final Keyword Trick
docs.text:
final int x = 10;
byte b = x;   // OK

Why?

Because x is compile-time constant within range of byte.

But:

int x = 10;
byte b = x;   // ERROR

Even though value same.

Interviewers love this.

docs.title: 5️⃣ Reference Type Casting
docs.text: There are two:

docs.title: Upcasting (Automatic)
docs.text:
Animal a = new Dog();

Child → Parent Safe No cast needed

docs.title: Downcasting (Explicit)
docs.text:
Animal a = new Dog();
Dog d = (Dog) a;

Parent → Child Needs cast May cause ClassCastException

docs.title: ⚠️ Dangerous Trap
docs.text:
Animal a = new Animal();
Dog d = (Dog) a;  // Compiles but runtime exception

docs.title: 6️⃣ How to Answer in Interview
docs.text:
If interviewer asks:

👉 “Explain type casting in Java.”

Answer:

In Java, type conversion happens either automatically (widening) when converting smaller types to larger types, or explicitly (narrowing) when converting larger types to smaller types which may cause data loss. Java also supports reference type casting through upcasting and downcasting.

docs.title: 📘 Notes Summary
docs.text:
• Widening → safe → automatic
  • Narrowing → data loss → manual cast
  • Arithmetic → smaller types become int
  • boolean → no conversion
  • Object casting → runtime type matters

docs.title: Question 1
docs.text:
int x = 5;
double y = x / 2;
System.out.println(y);

docs.title: Step-by-step:
docs.text:
• x is int (5)
  • 2 is int
  • So x / 2 → integer division
  • 5 / 2 = 2 (decimal part discarded)

Then:

  • double y = 2;
  • int 2 is widened to double → 2.0

docs.title: ✅ Output:
docs.text:
2.0

✔ It is integer division (fraction part removed)

docs.title: Question 2
docs.text:
double y = 5 / 2;
System.out.println(y);
  • 5 is int
  • 2 is int
  • division happens BEFORE assignment

So result?

👉 It is still integer division.

So:

5 / 2 = 2 Then 2 → converted to double → 2.0

docs.title: ✅ Output:
docs.text:
2.0

Many beginners say 2.5. That is wrong.

docs.title: If you want 2.5
docs.text:
You must make one operand double:

double y = 5 / 2.0;

or

double y = (double)5 / 2;

docs.title: Question 3
docs.text:
Why does Java promote byte/short to int in arithmetic?

This is important.

docs.title: Reason:
docs.text:
CPU works efficiently with at least 32-bit integers.

So Java promotes:

  • byte
  • short
  • char

to int before arithmetic operations.

This is called Integer Promotion Rule.

Example:

byte a = 10;
byte b = 20;
int c = a + b;   // promoted to int

Even though both are byte, addition happens in int.

System.out.println(5 / 2.0);
  • 2.0 is double
  • int 5 is promoted to double
  • double division → 2.5
System.out.println(5.0 / 2);
  • 5.0 is double
  • int 2 promoted to double
  • double division → 2.5
System.out.println(5.0 / 2.0);
  • both double
  • double division → 2.5

docs.title: 🔥 Golden Rule (Write in your notes)
docs.text:
If any one operand is double, the entire expression becomes double.

If both operands are int → integer division.

docs.title: Code
docs.text:
System.out.println(5 / 2 * 1.0);
System.out.println(5 * 1.0 / 2);

docs.title: 1️⃣ 5 / 2 * 1.0
docs.text:
Operator precedence:

  • / and * have same precedence
  • evaluated left to right

So:

Step 1:

5 / 2

Both int → integer division → 2

Step 2:

2 * 1.0

Now one operand is double → result becomes double → 2.0

✅ Output:

2.0

docs.title: 2️⃣ 5 * 1.0 / 2
docs.text:
Step 1:

5 * 1.0

int promoted to double → 5.0

Step 2:

5.0 / 2

double division → 2.5

✅ Output:

2.5

docs.title: 🔥 What Interviewer Tests Here
docs.text:
They test:

  • Order of evaluation
  • Type promotion
  • Integer division behavior
  • Expression evaluation left to right

docs.title: 📘 Write This Rule in Notes
docs.text: Arithmetic operators evaluate left to right. Type promotion happens at the time of operation, not at assignment.

docs.title: ✅ Why It Does NOT Compile
docs.text:
byte b = 100;
b = b + 1;

docs.title: Step-by-step:
docs.text:
• b is byte
  • 1 is int (default literal type)
  • In arithmetic, byte is promoted to int
  • So b + 1 becomes int
  • Cannot assign int to byte without casting

So compiler error:

possible lossy conversion from int to byte

docs.title: ✅ Correct Version
docs.text:
b = (byte) (b + 1);

Now it compiles.

docs.title: 🔥 Why Does This Compile?
docs.text:
byte b = 100;
b += 1;  // ✅ compiles!

Even though b = b + 1; does NOT compile, this b += 1; version DOES compile.

docs.title: ✅ Reason:
docs.text:
Java applies implicit cast in compound assignment (+=, -=, *=, etc.)

The compiler automatically inserts a cast, like:

b = (byte)(b + 1);

So:

"compiler resolves b += 1 as b = (byte)(b + 1);" ✅

docs.title: ⚠ Why Does This Rule Exist?
docs.text:
To make code cleaner:

  • Compound operators (+=, -=) were designed to allow implicit narrowing cast
  • But direct assignment (b = b + 1;) does not allow implicit cast
  • This is how Java maintains strict typing and convenient shorthand at the same time

docs.title: 📘 Write in Your Notes
docs.text:
byte b = 100;
b = b + 1;       // ❌ Compilation error (int → byte)
b += 1;          // ✅ Compiles (implicit cast)

Same applies to:

short s = 10;
s = s * 2;      // ❌
s *= 2;         // ✅

docs.title: 📚 Operators In Java
docs.text:
We will cover:

  1. Increment / Decrement (++, --)
  2. Short-circuit operators (&&, ||)
  3. Ternary operator (?:)
  4. Bitwise operators (&, |, ^, ~, <<, >>, >>>)

Step by step. No confusion.

docs.title: Two Types
docs.text:
• Pre-increment → ++x
  • Post-increment → x++

docs.title: Post-increment
docs.text:
int x = 5;
int y = x++;

Step-by-step:

  • y = 5
  • then x becomes 6

Final:

x = 6
y = 5

docs.title: Pre-increment
docs.text:
int x = 5;
int y = ++x;

Step-by-step:

  • x becomes 6
  • y = 6

Final:

x = 6
y = 6

docs.title: 🔥 Dangerous Interview Trap
docs.text:
Predict:

int x = 5;
System.out.println(x++ + ++x);

Solve carefully.

Step-by-step:

  1. x++ → 5 (then x becomes 6)
  2. ++x → x becomes 7, value 7
  3. 5 + 7 = 12

Final x = 7

Output:

12

⚠ Interview tip: Avoid writing such confusing code in real projects.

docs.title: Logical AND
docs.text:
if (a > 5 && b > 10)

If first condition is false → second NOT evaluated.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
int x = 5;
if (x > 10 && ++x > 6) {
}
System.out.println(x);

Since x > 10 is false → ++x never executes.

Output:

5

docs.title: Difference Between && and &
docs.text:
&& → short-circuit & → evaluates both sides always

Same for:

|| vs |

docs.title: 🔥 Interview Trap
docs.text:
int x = 0;
if (true || ++x > 0) {
}
System.out.println(x);

Output?

true || anything → second not evaluated

Output:

0

docs.title: 3️⃣ Ternary Operator (?:)
docs.text:
Syntax:

condition ? value1 : value2;

Example:

int x = 10;
String result = (x > 5) ? "Big" : "Small";

docs.title: 🔥 Trap
docs.text:
int x = 10;
System.out.println(x > 5 ? x++ : x--);
System.out.println(x);

Step-by-step:

  • condition true
  • prints 10 (post-increment)
  • x becomes 11

Final output:

10
11

docs.title: 4️⃣ Bitwise Operators
docs.text: Used at binary level.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
System.out.println(5 & 3);

Binary:

5 = 0101
3 = 0011
--------------
    0001 = 1

Output:

1

docs.title: Left Shift
docs.text:
System.out.println(5 << 1);

Binary:

0101 << 1 = 1010

Result:

10

Means: 5 * 2

docs.title: Right Shift
docs.text:
System.out.println(8 >> 1);

Binary:

1000 >> 1 = 0100

Result:

4

Means: 8 / 2

docs.title: Code
docs.text:
int x = 10;
int y = x++ + ++x + x++;
System.out.println(y);
System.out.println(x);

docs.title: Step-by-Step Execution (Left → Right Evaluation)
docs.text:
Java evaluates operands left to right.

Initial:

x = 10

docs.title: 1️⃣ First term → x++
docs.text:
Post-increment:

  • Use current value → 10
  • Then increment

After this:

Value used = 10
x = 11

docs.title: 2️⃣ Second term → ++x
docs.text:
Pre-increment:

  • Increment first → x = 12
  • Then use value → 12

After this:

Value used = 12
x = 12

docs.title: 3️⃣ Third term → x++
docs.text:
Post-increment:

  • Use current value → 12
  • Then increment

After this:

Value used = 12
x = 13

docs.title: Final Calculation
docs.text:
y = 10 + 12 + 12
y = 34

Final:

x = 13

docs.title: ✅ Output
docs.text:
34
13

docs.title: ⚠ Interview Insight
docs.text:
Interviewers use this to test:

  • Pre vs post increment understanding
  • Left-to-right evaluation
  • Side effects in expressions

And important:

👉 In real projects, never write such code. It reduces readability and increases bugs.

docs.title: Code
docs.text:
int x = 5;
int y = x++ + x++ + ++x;
System.out.println(y);
System.out.println(x);

docs.title: Step-by-step (Left → Right)
docs.text:
Initial:

x = 5

docs.title: 1️⃣ First term → x++
docs.text:
• Use value → 5
  • Then increment → x becomes 6

Used value = 5 x = 6

docs.title: 2️⃣ Second term → x++
docs.text:
• Use value → 6
  • Then increment → x becomes 7

Used value = 6 x = 7

docs.title: 3️⃣ Third term → ++x
docs.text:
• Increment first → x becomes 8
  • Use value → 8

Used value = 8 x = 8

docs.title: Final Calculation
docs.text:
y = 5 + 6 + 8
y = 19

Final:

x = 8

docs.title: ✅ Output
docs.text:
19
8

docs.title: 🔥 Interview Insight
docs.text:
If interviewer asks:

What is rule for expression evaluation in Java?

Answer:

Java evaluates expressions strictly from left to right. Side effects like post and pre-increment happen during evaluation in that order.

docs.title: Code
docs.text:
int x = 5;
System.out.println(x++ + x++ + x++);
System.out.println(x);

docs.title: Step-by-step (Left → Right)
docs.text:
Initial:

x = 5

docs.title: 1️⃣ First x++
docs.text:
• Use 5
  • Then x becomes 6

Used = 5 x = 6

docs.title: 2️⃣ Second x++
docs.text:
• Use 6
  • Then x becomes 7

Used = 6 x = 7

docs.title: 3️⃣ Third x++
docs.text:
• Use 7
  • Then x becomes 8

Used = 7 x = 8

docs.title: Final Calculation
docs.text:
5 + 6 + 7 = 18

Final:

x = 8

docs.title: ✅ Output
docs.text:
18
8

docs.title: 🧠 Important Interview Note
docs.text:
Java guarantees:

  • Left-to-right operand evaluation.
  • Post-increment returns old value, then increments.

This is defined behavior (unlike C/C++ where this can be undefined).

docs.title: Code
docs.text:
int x = 1;
int y = 1;

if (x++ == 1 && ++y == 2) {
    x++;
}

System.out.println(x);
System.out.println(y);

docs.title: Step-by-Step Execution
docs.text:
Initial:

x = 1
y = 1

docs.title: 1️⃣ Evaluate: x++ == 1
docs.text:
• x++ → use 1
  • then x becomes 2
  • 1 == 1 → true

Now:

x = 2
y = 1

Since first condition is true, second condition will be evaluated (&& needs both true).

docs.title: 2️⃣ Evaluate: ++y == 2
docs.text:
• ++y → y becomes 2
  • 2 == 2 → true

Now:

x = 2
y = 2

Both conditions true → enter if block.

docs.title: 3️⃣ Inside if:
docs.text:
x++;
  • Use 2
  • x becomes 3

Now:

x = 3
y = 2

docs.title: ✅ Final Output
docs.text:
3
2

docs.title: 🧠 What Interviewer Tests Here
docs.text:
• Post vs pre increment
  • Short-circuit &&
  • Evaluation order (left → right)
  • Side effects inside condition

docs.title: Code
docs.text:
```java id="zky2lk" int x = 1; int y = 1;

if (x++ == 2 && ++y == 2) { x++; }

System.out.println(x); System.out.println(y); 

---

## Step-by-step

Initial:

* x = 1
* y = 1

### 1️⃣ Evaluate `x++ == 2`

* `x++` uses 1, then x becomes 2
* `1 == 2` → **false**

Now:

* x = 2
* y = 1

### 2️⃣ Short-circuit happens

Because first part of `&&` is false, Java **will not evaluate** `++y == 2`.

So:

* y stays 1
* `if` block not executed

✅ Final output:

* 2
* 1

---

# Code

```java
int x = 10;
int y = 20;

int z = (x > y) ? x++ : y++;
System.out.println(z);
System.out.println(x);
System.out.println(y);

docs.title: Condition:
docs.text:
x > y

10 > 20 → false

So ternary chooses the false branch:

y++

docs.title: Evaluate y++
docs.text:
• Use current value → 20
  • Then increment → y becomes 21

So:

z = 20
y = 21

x was never touched.

docs.title: ✅ Final State
docs.text:
z = 20
x = 10
y = 21

docs.title: 🧠 Important Interview Insight
docs.text:
In ternary operator:

👉 Only ONE branch executes. 👉 The other branch is completely ignored.

Unlike if statements where confusion sometimes happens with side effects, ternary is strictly one branch execution.

docs.title: Code
docs.text:
int x = 5;
int y = 10;

System.out.println(x > y ? x++ : ++y);
System.out.println(x);
System.out.println(y);

docs.title: Step 1: Evaluate condition
docs.text:
x > y
5 > 10 → false

So ternary chooses false branch:

++y

docs.title: Step 2: Evaluate ++y
docs.text:
Pre-increment:

  • y becomes 11
  • value used = 11

So first print:

11

Now:

x = 5
y = 11

docs.title: Step 3: Print x and y
docs.text:
5
11

docs.title: ✅ Final Output
docs.text:
11
5
11

docs.title: 🧠 What Interviewer Is Testing
docs.text:
• Only one branch executes
  • Pre-increment changes value before usage
  • Order of evaluation

docs.title: Code
docs.text:
int x = 3;
int y = 4;

if ((x & 1) == 1 && (y++ > 3)) {
    x++;
}

System.out.println(x);
System.out.println(y);

docs.title: 1️⃣ Evaluate (x & 1) == 1
docs.text:
• x = 3 → binary 0011
  • 1 → binary 0001
  • 3 & 1 → 0001 → 1

So:

(x & 1) == 1  →  true

Meaning: x is odd (common trick to check odd/even).

docs.title: 2️⃣ Because first part is true, evaluate second part
docs.text:
(y++ > 3)

  • y++ uses current value 4
  • checks 4 > 3 → true
  • then y becomes 5

So the full && becomes:

true && true → true

So if block runs.

docs.title: 3️⃣ Inside if: x++;
docs.text:
x becomes 4.

Final:

  • x = 4
  • y = 5

✅ Output:

4
5

docs.title: 📘 Notes (Important Interview Lines)
docs.text:
• x & 1 checks odd/even:

  • 1 → odd

  • 0 → even
  • && short-circuits, but here it didn’t short-circuit because first condition was true.
  • y++ changes y even if condition is true/false depending on whether that side executes.
int x = 0;
int y = 0;

if (x++ == 0 & y++ == 0) { }
System.out.println(x);
System.out.println(y);

docs.title: Key point: & is NOT short-circuit
docs.text: Even in boolean expressions, single & evaluates both sides.

docs.title: Left side: x++ == 0
docs.text:
• uses x=0 → true
  • then x becomes 1

docs.title: Right side: y++ == 0
docs.text:
• still evaluated because &
  • uses y=0 → true
  • then y becomes 1

So:

  • x = 1
  • y = 1

docs.title: 📘 Notes line (important)
docs.text:
• && short-circuit: may skip right side
  • & evaluates both sides always (even in boolean)

docs.title: 1️⃣ What they do (Interview style)
docs.text:
• << Left shift: shifts bits left, fills right with 0
  • >> Signed right shift: shifts bits right, fills left with sign bit (0 for +, 1 for -)
  • >>> Unsigned right shift: shifts bits right, fills left with 0 always

docs.title: For positive numbers:
docs.text: • >> and >>> behave the same (both fill with 0)

docs.title: For negative numbers:
docs.text:
• >> keeps negative sign (fills 1 on left)
  • >>> makes it “look positive” (fills 0 on left)

docs.title: 5 << 1
docs.text:
5 in binary (8-bit view): 00000101 Shift left 1 → 00001010 = 10

So:

5 << 1  => 10

docs.title: 8 >> 1
docs.text:
8: 00001000 Right shift 1 → 00000100 = 4

So:

8 >> 1  => 4

docs.title: 4️⃣ The BIG Interview Trap: negative numbers
Example: -4 >> 1
docs.text:
Two’s complement (conceptually):

  • -4 ends with bits ...11111100

Signed right shift >> 1 fills with 1 (sign bit):

  • result becomes ...11111110 which is -2

✅ So:

-4 >> 1   => -2

docs.title: Example: -4 >>> 1
docs.text:
Unsigned right shift fills with 0:

  • left side becomes 0-filled
  • result becomes a large positive int

✅ So:

-4 >>> 1  => 2147483646

Why that number?

  • For int (32-bit), -4 is 0xFFFFFFFC
  • >>> 1 → 0x7FFFFFFE = 2147483646

docs.title: 5️⃣ Another common trap: shift count masking
docs.text:
Java masks the shift distance:

  • For int: only lower 5 bits used → n & 31 (0–31)
  • For long: only lower 6 bits used → n & 63 (0–63)

So:

1 << 32   // same as 1 << (32 & 31) = 1 << 0 = 1

This surprises many people.

docs.title: 6️⃣ When shifts act like multiply/divide
docs.text:
• x << 1 ≈ x * 2
  • x >> 1 ≈ x / 2 (for positives)

⚠ For negatives, >> is like divide-by-2 with rounding toward negative infinity behavior depending on bits. Safer to say: “it shifts bits; division equivalence is not always safe for negatives.”

docs.title: ✅ Bitwise shift “How to answer in interview”
docs.text: << shifts left and fills zeros. >> shifts right and preserves the sign bit (arithmetic shift). >>> shifts right and fills zeros (logical shift), so for negative numbers it produces a large positive value.

docs.title: 📘 Operator Precedence Table (Interview-focused)
docs.text:
Highest → Lowest (common ones)

  1. Postfix: expr++ expr-- () [] .
  2. Unary: ++expr --expr + - ! ~ (type)
  3. Multiplicative: * / %
  4. Additive: + -
  5. Shift: << >> >>>
  6. Relational: < <= > >= instanceof
  7. Equality: == !=
  8. Bitwise AND: &
  9. Bitwise XOR: ^
  10. Bitwise OR: |
  11. Logical AND: &&
  12. Logical OR: ||
  13. Ternary: ?:
  14. Assignment: = += -= *= /= &= |= ^= <<= >>= >>>=

docs.title: Golden note:
docs.text: • Same precedence operators evaluate left to right (except assignments are right to left)

docs.title: Trap 1: + vs <<
docs.text:
System.out.println(1 << 2 + 1);

+ happens before << So it becomes 1 << 3 = 8

docs.title: Trap 2: & vs ==
docs.text:
System.out.println(1 & 2 == 0);

== happens before &? Actually: & has lower precedence than ==, so 2 == 0 evaluated first? Wait carefully:

  • Precedence: Equality (==) is higher than bitwise AND (&) ✅ So expression becomes:
1 & (2 == 0)  // invalid: int & boolean

➡️ This does NOT compile.

Correct way:

System.out.println((1 & 2) == 0); // true

docs.title: ✅ Your Notes Snapshot (copy-paste)
docs.text:
• >> keeps sign bit, >>> fills 0
  • -4 >> 1 = -2
  • -4 >>> 1 = 2147483646
  • int shift distance masked by & 31

docs.title: ✅ Question 1
docs.text:
System.out.println(1 << 2 + 1);

You answered 8 ✔ Correct.

Because:

  • 2 + 1 = 3
  • 1 << 3 = 8

docs.title: ✅ Question 2
docs.text:
System.out.println(-1 >>> 1);

Let me explain quickly and finish this topic cleanly.

  • -1 in 32-bit binary = all 1s
11111111 11111111 11111111 11111111
  • >>> 1 shifts right and fills left with 0

So result becomes:

01111111 11111111 11111111 11111111

That equals:

2147483647

So answer is:

2147483647

docs.title: ✅ Operators Topic – Completed
docs.text:
You now clearly understand:

  • Pre vs Post increment
  • Short-circuit vs &
  • Ternary
  • Bitwise basics
  • Shift traps
  • Precedence basics

That’s strong.

docs.title: 📚 Control Flow In Java
docs.text:
We’ll cover:

1️⃣ if / else 2️⃣ switch 3️⃣ Loops (for, while, do-while) 4️⃣ break, continue 5️⃣ Common Interview Traps

docs.title: Basic
docs.text:
if (condition) {
   // code
}

Condition must be boolean.

⚠ Java does NOT allow:

if (1)   // ❌ Not allowed (C allows this)

docs.title: 🔥 Interview Trap 1
docs.text:
int x = 5;
if (x = 10) { }  // ❌

Why error?

Because:

  • x = 10 returns int
  • if expects boolean

In C it works. In Java it doesn’t.

docs.title: 🔥 Interview Trap 2: No Braces
docs.text:
int x = 5;
if (x > 3)
    System.out.println("Hi");
    System.out.println("Hello");

Output:

Hi
Hello

Because only first statement is inside if.

Always use {} in real projects.

docs.title: Basic
docs.text:
int x = 2;

switch(x) {
    case 1:
        System.out.println("One");
        break;
    case 2:
        System.out.println("Two");
        break;
}

docs.title: 🔥 Trap: Fall-through
docs.text:
int x = 1;

switch(x) {
    case 1:
        System.out.println("One");
    case 2:
        System.out.println("Two");
}

Output:

One
Two

Because no break.

docs.title: 🔥 Trap: Valid Types
docs.text:
Switch supports:

  • byte
  • short
  • char
  • int
  • String
  • enum

❌ Not allowed:

  • long
  • float
  • double
  • boolean

docs.title: 🔥 Trap: Duplicate case
docs.text:
final int x = 1;
switch(1) {
    case x:  // allowed (compile-time constant)
}

But:

int x = 1;
case x;  // ❌ not constant

docs.title: FOR Loop
docs.text:
for(int i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
   System.out.println(i);
}

docs.title: 🔥 Trap
docs.text:
for(;;) {
   System.out.println("Hi");
}

Infinite loop.

All 3 parts optional.

docs.title: WHILE
docs.text:
while(condition) {
}

docs.title: DO-WHILE
docs.text:
Runs at least once.

do {
   System.out.println("Hi");
} while(false);

Output:

Hi

docs.title: Break
docs.text: Stops loop.

docs.title: Continue
docs.text: Skips current iteration.

docs.title: 🔥 Trap
docs.text:
for(int i = 0; i < 5; i++) {
    if(i == 2) continue;
    System.out.print(i);
}

Output:

0134

docs.title: 5️⃣ Labelled Break (Interview Trap)
docs.text:
outer:
for(int i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
    for(int j = 0; j < 3; j++) {
        break outer;
    }
}

Exits both loops.

docs.title: ✅ Question 1 – Dangling Else
docs.text:
int x = 0;
if (x > 0)
    if (x < 10)
        System.out.println("A");
    else
        System.out.println("B");

docs.title: Rule:
docs.text:
👉 In Java, else always matches the nearest unmatched if.

So else belongs to:

if (x < 10)

This is called the Dangling Else Problem.

In real code → always use braces {}.

docs.title: ✅ Question 2 – While with Semicolon
docs.text:
int x = 0;
while(x++ < 3);
System.out.println(x);

Notice:

while(x++ < 3);   // <-- semicolon

This is an empty loop.

Step-by-step:

  • x=0 → 0<3 → true → x=1
  • x=1 → 1<3 → true → x=2
  • x=2 → 2<3 → true → x=3
  • x=3 → 3<3 → false → x=4 (because post-increment happens)

Loop exits.

Final x = 4

Correct answer: 4

docs.title: ✅ Question 3 – For Loop with Print in Update
docs.text:
for(int i = 0; i < 3; System.out.print(i++));

Execution order in for:

Initialization
Condition
Body
Update

But body is empty because of semicolon.

So:

  • i=0 → condition true → update prints 0 (i becomes 1)
  • i=1 → condition true → update prints 1 (i becomes 2)
  • i=2 → condition true → update prints 2 (i becomes 3)
  • i=3 → condition false → stop

Output:

012

Correct.

docs.title: 🧠 You Are Strong in Control Flow
docs.text:
You are thinking clearly about:

  • Semicolon traps
  • Post increment behavior
  • Loop evaluation order
  • Dangling else

This is very good sign.

docs.title: ✅ Question 4 – Dangling Else
docs.text:
int x = 0;
if (x > 0)
    if (x < 10)
        System.out.println("A");
    else
        System.out.println("B");

docs.title: Rule:
docs.text:
👉 In Java, else always matches the nearest unmatched if.

So else belongs to:

if (x < 10)

This is called the Dangling Else Problem.

In real code → always use braces {}.

docs.title: ✅ Question 5 – While with Semicolon
docs.text:
int x = 0;
while(x++ < 3);
System.out.println(x);

Notice:

while(x++ < 3);   // <-- semicolon

This is an empty loop.

Step-by-step:

  • x=0 → 0<3 → true → x=1
  • x=1 → 1<3 → true → x=2
  • x=2 → 2<3 → true → x=3
  • x=3 → 3<3 → false → x=4 (because post-increment happens)

Loop exits.

Final x = 4

Correct answer: 4

docs.title: ✅ Question 6 – For Loop with Print in Update
docs.text:
for(int i = 0; i < 3; System.out.print(i++));

Execution order in for:

Initialization
Condition
Body
Update

But body is empty because of semicolon.

So:

  • i=0 → condition true → update prints 0 (i becomes 1)
  • i=1 → condition true → update prints 1 (i becomes 2)
  • i=2 → condition true → update prints 2 (i becomes 3)
  • i=3 → condition false → stop

Output:

012

Correct.

docs.title: 🧠 You Are Strong in Control Flow
docs.text:
You are thinking clearly about:

  • Semicolon traps
  • Post increment behavior
  • Loop evaluation order
  • Dangling else

This is very good sign.

docs.title: ✅ Next: Nested Loops (Very Important)
docs.text:
Interviewers use nested loops to test:

  • Understanding of iteration
  • Dry run ability
  • Time complexity thinking
  • Pattern logic

docs.title: 📘 1️⃣ Basic Nested Loop
docs.text:
for(int i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
    for(int j = 0; j < 3; j++) {
        System.out.print(i + "" + j + " ");
    }
}

docs.title: 📘 2️⃣ Classic Pattern Trap
docs.text:
Predict output:

for(int i = 1; i <= 3; i++) {
    for(int j = 1; j <= i; j++) {
        System.out.print("*");
    }
    System.out.println();
}

Think step-by-step.

docs.title: 📘 3️⃣ Time Complexity Insight
docs.text:
Nested loops like:

for(int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    for(int j = 0; j < n; j++) {
    }
}

Time complexity = O(n²)

But this:

for(int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    for(int j = 0; j < i; j++) {
    }
}

Time complexity = 1 + 2 + 3 + ... + n = n(n+1)/2 = O(n²) (still quadratic)

Interviewers love asking this.

Now let’s quickly verify like interview dry-run style.

docs.title: ✅ Pattern 1
docs.text:
for(int i = 1; i <= 3; i++) {
    for(int j = 1; j <= i; j++) {
        System.out.print("*");
    }
    System.out.println();
}

i = 1 → print 1 star i = 2 → print 2 stars i = 3 → print 3 stars

So:

*
**
***

Correct.

docs.title: ✅ Pattern 2
docs.text:
for(int i = 3; i >= 1; i--) {
    for(int j = 1; j <= i; j++) {
        System.out.print(j);
    }
    System.out.println();
}

i = 3 → 1 2 3 i = 2 → 1 2 i = 1 → 1

So:

123
12
1

Correct.

docs.title: 🧠 Interview Insight
docs.text:
Nested loops are testing:

  • Control of outer loop
  • Resetting inner loop each iteration
  • Understanding loop boundaries

docs.title: Let’s Confirm (Interview Dry Run)
docs.text:
for(int i = 1; i <= 3; i++) {
    for(int j = i; j <= 3; j++) {
        System.out.print(j);
    }
    System.out.println();
}

docs.title: i = 1
docs.text:
j starts at 1 → prints 1 2 3

123

docs.title: i = 2
docs.text:
j starts at 2 → prints 2 3

23

docs.title: i = 3
docs.text:
j starts at 3 → prints 3

3

docs.title: 🧠 Why This Is Important
docs.text:
Interviewers use this to test:

  • Inner loop start value depends on outer loop
  • Changing bounds changes pattern
  • Understanding triangular iteration (used in DSA often)

docs.title: Code
docs.text:
for(int i = 1; i <= 3; i++) {
    for(int j = 1; j <= 3; j++) {
        if(i == j)
            break;
        System.out.print(i + "" + j + " ");
    }
}

docs.title: i = 1
docs.text: • j = 1 → i == j → break (no print)

docs.title: i = 2
docs.text:
• j = 1 → i != j → print 21
  • j = 2 → i == j → break

docs.title: i = 3
docs.text:
• j = 1 → print 31
  • j = 2 → print 32
  • j = 3 → break

✅ Output:

21 31 32

docs.title: Let’s Dry Run (Interview Style)
docs.text:
for(int i = 1; i <= 3; i++) {
    for(int j = 1; j <= 3; j++) {
        if(i == j)
            continue;
        System.out.print(i + "" + j + " ");
    }
}

docs.title: i = 1
docs.text:
• j = 1 → skip (continue)
  • j = 2 → print 12
  • j = 3 → print 13

docs.title: i = 2
docs.text:
• j = 1 → print 21
  • j = 2 → skip
  • j = 3 → print 23

docs.title: i = 3
docs.text:
• j = 1 → print 31
  • j = 2 → print 32
  • j = 3 → skip

✅ Final Output:

12 13 21 23 31 32

docs.title: 🧠 Key Difference (Interview Gold)
docs.text: break continue exits loop skips current iteration stops inner loop completely moves to next iteration

docs.title: 📚 Modern Switch
docs.text:
Now we move to Switch Advanced + Modern Switch (Java 14+)

This topic is important for interviews because many candidates only know old switch.

docs.title: 📘 1️⃣ Traditional Switch (Old Style)
docs.text:
Basic syntax:

switch(expression) {
    case value1:
        // code
        break;
    case value2:
        // code
        break;
    default:
        // code
}

docs.title: 1️⃣ Expression Type Must Be:
docs.text:
Allowed:

  • byte
  • short
  • char
  • int
  • String
  • enum

❌ Not allowed:

  • long
  • float
  • double
  • boolean

docs.title: 🔥 Trap 1: Fall-Through
docs.text:
int x = 2;

switch(x) {
    case 1:
        System.out.println("One");
    case 2:
        System.out.println("Two");
    case 3:
        System.out.println("Three");
}

docs.title: Output:
docs.text:
Two
Three

Because no break.

docs.title: 🔥 Trap 2: Order of Cases
docs.text:
Cases can be in any order.

switch(x) {
    case 5:
    case 1:
    case 3:
}

Valid.

docs.title: 🔥 Trap 3: Case Must Be Compile-Time Constant
docs.text:
int a = 5;
switch(5) {
    case a:   // ❌ ERROR
}

But:

final int a = 5;
case a:   // ✅ allowed

Because final primitive constant is compile-time constant.

docs.title: 📘 2️⃣ Switch with String
docs.text:
String day = "MON";

switch(day) {
    case "MON":
        System.out.println("Start");
        break;
}

Internally, Java uses hashcode comparison.

docs.title: 📘 3️⃣ Modern Switch (Java 14+)
docs.text: This is very important for modern interviews.

docs.title: ✅ Switch Expression (Arrow Style)
docs.text:
int day = 2;

String result = switch(day) {
    case 1 -> "Monday";
    case 2 -> "Tuesday";
    default -> "Other";
};

System.out.println(result);

No break needed.

docs.title: 🔥 Key Difference
docs.text:
Old switch:

  • statement
  • needs break
  • fall-through possible

New switch:

  • expression
  • no fall-through
  • returns value
  • cleaner syntax

docs.title: 📘 4️⃣ Multiple Cases in One Line (Modern)
docs.text:
int day = 2;

String result = switch(day) {
    case 1, 2, 3 -> "Weekday";
    case 6, 7 -> "Weekend";
    default -> "Other";
};

docs.title: 📘 5️⃣ Block Style in Modern Switch
docs.text:
int day = 2;

String result = switch(day) {
    case 2 -> {
        System.out.println("Processing...");
        yield "Tuesday";
    }
    default -> "Other";
};

yield is required when using block.

docs.title: ✅ Question 1
docs.text:
int x = 1;

switch(x) {
    case 1:
        System.out.println("A");
    default:
        System.out.println("B");
    case 2:
        System.out.println("C");
}

docs.title: Execution:
docs.text:
• x = 1 → case 1 matches
  • Print A
  • No break → fall through
  • default → print B
  • case 2 → print C

✅ Output:

A
B
C

docs.title: Question 2
docs.text:
int x = 1;

String result = switch(x) {
    case 1 -> "A";
    case 2 -> "B";
};

System.out.println(result);

docs.title: The Real Reason
docs.text:
Modern switch (switch expression) must be exhaustive.

That means:

  • It must cover all possible values
  • Or have a default

Since int can be many values (not just 1 and 2), compiler says:

"Switch expression does not cover all possible input values"

So it does NOT compile.

docs.title: Correct Version
docs.text:
String result = switch(x) {
    case 1 -> "A";
    case 2 -> "B";
    default -> "Other";
};

Now it compiles.

docs.title: 🧠 Interview Insight
docs.text:
Old switch:

  • default optional

New switch expression:

  • must be exhaustive
  • especially for int

BUT:

If switching on enum and covering all enum constants, default is not required.

Example:

enum Day { MON, TUE }

String result = switch(day) {
    case MON -> "Start";
    case TUE -> "Next";
};

This compiles because enum values are fully covered.

docs.title: 🧠 Important Interview Insight
docs.text:
Modern switch:

  • Multiple values per case allowed (case 1, 2)
  • No fall-through
  • Must be exhaustive
  • Cleaner and safer than old switch

docs.title: ✅ Control Flow Topic Status
docs.text:
You now understand:

  • if / else (dangling else)
  • loops (semicolon traps)
  • nested loops
  • break vs continue
  • traditional switch
  • modern switch expression
  • exhaustiveness rule

That is strong foundation.

docs.title: 📚 Methods & Stack Frames
docs.text:
We will cover:

1️⃣ What happens when a method is called 2️⃣ Stack memory structure 3️⃣ Stack Frame creation 4️⃣ Pass-by-value (big confusion area) 5️⃣ Recursion stack behavior 6️⃣ Interview traps

docs.title: 1️⃣ What Happens When a Method is Called?
docs.text:
Example:

public class Test {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        int x = 5;
        int result = add(x);
        System.out.println(result);
    }

    static int add(int num) {
        return num + 10;
    }
}

docs.title: 🧠 Step 1: Program starts
docs.text:
• JVM creates main thread
  • Stack created for main thread
  • main() stack frame pushed

docs.title: 🧠 Step 2: x = 5
docs.text:
main()
  x = 5

docs.title: 🧠 Step 3: add(x) is called
docs.text:
• New stack frame created for add()
  • Parameter num receives copy of x

Important: 👉 Java is pass-by-value 👉 num gets copy of 5

docs.title: 🧠 Step 4: return num + 10
docs.text:
• 15 returned
  • add() frame destroyed (popped)

docs.title: 🧠 Step 5: Print result
docs.text:
Output:

15

docs.title: 2️⃣ What is a Stack Frame?
docs.text:
Every method call creates:

  • Local variables
  • Parameters
  • Return address
  • Operand stack

Stored inside a stack frame.

When method finishes: 👉 Frame is popped 👉 Memory automatically cleaned

docs.title: 3️⃣ Important Rule
docs.text:
Each thread has:

  • Its own stack
  • Multiple stack frames

Stack is LIFO (Last In First Out).

docs.title: 4️⃣ Big Interview Question: Is Java Pass-by-Reference?
docs.text:
Answer:

❌ NO ✔ Java is always pass-by-value

Even for objects.

Example:

static void change(int x) {
    x = 100;
}

public static void main(String[] args) {
    int a = 10;
    change(a);
    System.out.println(a);
}

Output:

10

Because:

  • Copy of 10 passed
  • Original not changed

docs.title: 5️⃣ Object Pass-by-Value Trap
docs.text:
class Person {
    int age;
}

static void change(Person p) {
    p.age = 50;
}

public static void main(String[] args) {
    Person obj = new Person();
    obj.age = 20;
    change(obj);
    System.out.println(obj.age);
}

Output:

50

Why?

Because:

  • Copy of reference passed
  • Both point to same object
  • Object modified

But if:

static void change(Person p) {
    p = new Person();
    p.age = 100;
}

Then output:

20

Because:

  • Only local copy changed
  • Original reference unchanged

docs.title: 🔥 Now I Test Your Understanding
docs.text:
Predict output:

static void modify(int a) {
    a = a + 5;
}

static void modifyArray(int[] arr) {
    arr[0] = 100;
}

public static void main(String[] args) {
    int x = 10;
    modify(x);
    System.out.println(x);

    int[] nums = {1, 2, 3};
    modifyArray(nums);
    System.out.println(nums[0]);
}

✅ Output:

10
100

Now the interview-level explanation (short but deep):

docs.title: 1️⃣ modify(x)
docs.text:
• x is a primitive (int)
  • Java passes a copy of the value (10) into a
  • Inside method, a becomes 15, but that’s only the local copy
  • x in main stays 10

So first print: 10

docs.title: 2️⃣ modifyArray(nums)
docs.text:
• nums is a reference variable
  • Java passes a copy of the reference
  • Both arr (inside method) and nums (in main) point to the same array object in heap
  • Changing arr[0] changes the same heap array

So second print: 100

docs.title: 📘 Notes Line (Most Important)
docs.text:
✅ Java is pass-by-value always.

  • For primitives: value copy
  • For objects/arrays: reference copy (so object can be mutated)

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
Person p = new Person(20);

changeRef(p);
System.out.println(p.age);

changeObj(p);
System.out.println(p.age);

docs.title: 1️⃣ changeRef(p)
docs.text:
static void changeRef(Person p) {
    p = new Person(99);
}

docs.title: What happens?
docs.text:
• A copy of reference is passed
  • Inside method, p now points to a NEW object
  • But original reference in main still points to old object

So after method:

main → p → Person(age=20)

Output:

20

docs.title: 2️⃣ changeObj(p)
docs.text:
static void changeObj(Person p) {
    p.age = 99;
}

docs.title: What happens?
docs.text:
• Copy of reference passed
  • Both references point to SAME object
  • Modifying p.age modifies heap object

So:

main → p → Person(age=99)

Output:

99

docs.title: 🧠 Interview-Level Explanation (Memorize This)
docs.text:
Java is strictly pass-by-value. When passing objects, the value passed is a copy of the reference. Therefore, methods can modify the object’s state but cannot change the original reference.

This line alone can impress interviewers.

docs.title: 🔥 Now Stack Frame Deep Test
docs.text:
Predict output:

static void fun(int x) {
    x = x + 1;
    System.out.println(x);
}

public static void main(String[] args) {
    int x = 5;
    fun(x);
    System.out.println(x);
}

✅ Correct Output:

6
5

docs.title: Why (quick stack-frame reasoning)
docs.text:
• fun(x) receives a copy of x (5)
  • inside fun, local x becomes 6 → prints 6
  • main’s x stays 5 → prints 5

docs.title: 📘 Stack Frames + Recursion (Next)
docs.text: Now we move to recursion because it’s the clearest way to see stack frames.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
static void f(int n) {
    if (n == 0) return;
    System.out.print(n + " ");
    f(n - 1);
    System.out.print(n + " ");
}

public static void main(String[] args) {
    f(3);
}

✅ Correct Output:

3 2 1 1 2 3

Now let’s make it crystal clear with stack-frame thinking (this is what interviewers love).

docs.title: Why this output happens
docs.text:
f(3):
 print 3
  f(2):
   print 2
    f(1):
     print 1
      f(0): return
     print 1   // after returning
   print 2
 print 3

So:

  • First half prints while going down the recursion: 3 2 1
  • Second half prints while coming back up: 1 2 3

docs.title: 📘 Notes (One line)
docs.text:
Recursion has two phases:

  • Forward (call) phase
  • Backtracking (return) phase

docs.title: 🔥 Next Recursion + Stack Frame Trap
docs.text:
Predict output:

static int g(int n) {
    if (n == 0) return 0;
    return n + g(n - 1);
}

public static void main(String[] args) {
    System.out.println(g(3));
}

✅ Correct Output.

6

g(3) computes:

  • g(3) = 3 + g(2)
  • g(2) = 2 + g(1)
  • g(1) = 1 + g(0)
  • g(0) = 0

So total = 3 + 2 + 1 + 0 = 6.

docs.title: 🔥 Next: Stack Overflow + Recursion Depth (Interview)
docs.text:
If interviewer asks:

“Why does recursion cause StackOverflowError?”

You answer:

Each recursive call creates a new stack frame. If recursion is too deep (or has no base case), the call stack grows until it exceeds the stack memory limit, causing StackOverflowError.

docs.title: Final Method + Stack Frame Trap (Super Common)
docs.text:
Predict output:

static void h(int n) {
    if (n == 0) return;
    h(n - 1);
    System.out.print(n + " ");
}

public static void main(String[] args) {
    h(3);
}

✅ Correct Output:

1 2 3

Reason (quick, interview-style):

  • h(3) calls h(2) calls h(1) calls h(0) return
  • Printing happens after the recursive call returns, so it prints in reverse order while the stack unwinds: 1 2 3

docs.title: ✅ Methods & Stack Frames Notes — Completed Core
docs.text:
You now fully understand:

  • Stack vs stack frame
  • Method call frame creation & pop
  • Pass-by-value (primitive + reference)
  • Recursion call stack (forward + backward phase)

docs.title: Definition (Interview Style)
docs.text: Method overloading means defining multiple methods in the same class with the same name but different parameter lists.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
class MathUtil {
    int add(int a, int b) {
        return a + b;
    }

    double add(double a, double b) {
        return a + b;
    }

    int add(int a, int b, int c) {
        return a + b + c;
    }
}

Same method name → different parameters.

docs.title: Rules for Overloading
docs.text:
Must differ by:

  • Number of parameters
  • Type of parameters
  • Order of parameters

❌ Return type alone cannot differentiate.

Wrong:

int add(int a, int b) { }
double add(int a, int b) { }  // ❌ compile error

docs.title: When Does Overloading Get Resolved?
docs.text:
👉 At compile time

This is called:

Compile-time polymorphism

docs.title: 🔥 Overloading Interview Trap
docs.text:
Predict output:

class Test {
    void show(int a) {
        System.out.println("int");
    }

    void show(long a) {
        System.out.println("long");
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Test t = new Test();
        t.show(10);
    }
}

What prints?

Answer: int

Because:

  • 10 is int literal
  • Exact match preferred

docs.title: Definition (Interview Style)
docs.text: Method overriding occurs when a subclass provides a specific implementation of a method already defined in its parent class.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
class Animal {
    void sound() {
        System.out.println("Animal sound");
    }
}

class Dog extends Animal {
    @Override
    void sound() {
        System.out.println("Dog barks");
    }
}

docs.title: When Is Overriding Resolved?
docs.text:
👉 At runtime

This is called:

Runtime polymorphism Dynamic method dispatch

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Animal a = new Dog();
a.sound();

Output:

Dog barks

Because:

  • Method call decided at runtime
  • Based on object type (Dog)

docs.title: 🔥 Overriding Rules
docs.text:
• Method signature must be same
  • Cannot reduce access modifier
  • Return type must be same or covariant
  • Cannot override static method
  • Cannot override final method
  • Private methods are not overridden

docs.title: 🔥 Very Common Interview Trap
docs.text:
Predict output:

class Parent {
    static void show() {
        System.out.println("Parent");
    }
}

class Child extends Parent {
    static void show() {
        System.out.println("Child");
    }
}

public class Test {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Parent p = new Child();
        p.show();
    }
}

✅ Output:

Parent

Now let’s deeply understand why — this is a very common interview trap.

docs.title: Why It Prints "Parent"
docs.text:
Parent p = new Child();
p.show();

Key point:

👉 show() is static

Static methods:

  • Belong to the class
  • Not to the object
  • Are resolved at compile time
  • Are not overridden — they are hidden

This is called:

Method Hiding

docs.title: What happens internally?
docs.text:
Compiler looks at:

Parent p

Since reference type is Parent, compiler binds method call to:

Parent.show();

It does NOT check the object type (new Child()).

So output:

Parent

docs.title: 🧠 Interview Answer Line
docs.text:
Static methods are not overridden. They are hidden. Method call is resolved at compile time based on reference type.

That one sentence can impress interviewer.

docs.title: 🔥 Now Slightly Harder Trap
docs.text:
Predict output:

class Parent {
    void show() {
        System.out.println("Parent");
    }
}

class Child extends Parent {
    @Override
    void show() {
        System.out.println("Child");
    }
}

public class Test {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Parent p = new Child();
        p.show();
    }
}

✅ Correct.

Child

docs.title: Why?
docs.text:
Because this time show() is not static.

Parent p = new Child();
p.show();

For non-static methods:

  • Java uses dynamic method dispatch
  • Method is chosen at runtime
  • Based on the actual object type
  • Not the reference type

Here:

  • Reference type → Parent
  • Object type → Child

So JVM calls:

Child.show()

docs.title: 🔥 Interview Comparison (Very Important Table)
docs.text: Feature Overloading Overriding Class Same class Parent → Child Signature Must differ Must be same Return type Can differ (but not alone) Same or covariant Access modifier Any Cannot reduce visibility Resolution time Compile-time Runtime Static methods Can overload Cannot override (hidden)

docs.title: 🔥 Final Boss Trap (Most Asked)
docs.text:
Predict output:

class Parent {
    void show() {
        System.out.println("Parent show");
    }

    void display() {
        System.out.println("Parent display");
    }
}

class Child extends Parent {
    @Override
    void show() {
        System.out.println("Child show");
    }

    void display(int x) {
        System.out.println("Child display");
    }
}

public class Test {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Parent p = new Child();
        p.show();
        p.display();
    }
}

✅ Correct.

Child show
Parent display

Now let’s break it down like an interviewer expects.

docs.title: Code Summary
docs.text:
Parent p = new Child();
p.show();
p.display();

docs.title: 1️⃣ p.show()
docs.text:
• show() is overridden in Child
  • Non-static method
  • Runtime polymorphism applies
  • Object type is Child

So JVM calls:

Child show

docs.title: 2️⃣ p.display()
docs.text:
Now look carefully:

In Parent:

void display()

In Child:

void display(int x)

That is NOT overriding.

That is overloading.

The method signature is different.

So for:

p.display();

Compiler checks reference type (Parent).

Only method available is:

Parent.display()

So it prints:

Parent display

docs.title: 🧠 Interview Insight
docs.text:
This tests:

  • Overriding vs overloading difference
  • Method resolution based on reference type vs object type
  • Signature matching rule

docs.title: 🚀 Ultra Important Final Trap (Real Interview Favorite)
docs.text:
Predict output:

class Parent {
    Parent() {
        System.out.println("Parent constructor");
    }

    void show() {
        System.out.println("Parent show");
    }
}

class Child extends Parent {
    Child() {
        System.out.println("Child constructor");
    }

    @Override
    void show() {
        System.out.println("Child show");
    }
}

public class Test {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Parent p = new Child();
        p.show();
    }
}

✅ Output (in order):

Parent constructor
Child constructor
Child show

docs.title: Why (quick but interview-strong)
docs.text:
• new Child() triggers constructor chaining:

  • Parent() runs first via implicit super()

  • then Child() runs
  • After object creation, p.show() is a virtual (overridden) method, so it calls Child.show() at runtime.

docs.title: Notebook lines (copy-paste)
docs.text:
• Constructors are not overridden; they run in parent → child order.
  • Overridden methods are resolved at runtime based on object type.

docs.title: Next trap (last one, very common): Overloading with null
docs.text:
Predict output:

class Test {
    void print(String s) { System.out.println("String"); }
    void print(Object o) { System.out.println("Object"); }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Test t = new Test();
        t.print(null);
    }
}

✅ Output — it prints:

String

docs.title: Why (interview-ready)
docs.text: null can match both String and Object, but Java chooses the most specific overload. String is more specific than Object, so print(String) is selected at compile time.

docs.title: 🔥 Extra trap (very common follow-up)
docs.text:
What happens here?

class Test {
    void print(String s) { System.out.println("String"); }
    void print(Integer i) { System.out.println("Integer"); }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new Test().print(null);
    }
}

Does it compile? If yes, what prints? If not, why?

It does not compile because the compiler finds the method call ambiguous.

docs.title: Why It Fails
docs.text:
void print(String s)
void print(Integer i)

new Test().print(null);

null can match:

  • String
  • Integer

But now:

  • Neither String is subclass of Integer
  • Nor Integer is subclass of String

So compiler cannot decide which is more specific.

Result:

reference to print is ambiguous

Compilation error.

docs.title: 🧠 Interview Insight (Very Important Rule)
docs.text:
When overloading:

  1. Compiler chooses method at compile time
  2. It looks for the most specific match
  3. If two methods are equally specific → ambiguity error

docs.title: 🔥 Final Overloading Trap (Hard Level)
docs.text:
Predict:

class Test {

    void print(Object o) { System.out.println("Object"); }
    void print(String s) { System.out.println("String"); }
    void print(StringBuilder sb) { System.out.println("StringBuilder"); }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new Test().print(null);
    }
}

Does it compile? If yes, what prints? If not, why?

It does not compile because the compiler finds the method call ambiguous.

docs.title: Why it does NOT compile
docs.text:
We have overloads:

print(Object)
print(String)
print(StringBuilder)

Calling:

print(null)

null can match all reference types, so candidates are:

  • String
  • StringBuilder
  • Object

Java picks the most specific method.

  • Object is least specific → eliminated

  • Now between String and StringBuilder:

  • They are siblings (both extend Object, neither extends the other)

So neither is more specific → compiler cannot choose → ambiguous.

✅ Compile-time error similar to:

reference to print is ambiguous

docs.title: Notes (1-liner for interviews)
docs.text: print(null) chooses the most specific overload; if multiple unrelated reference types are equally specific (like String and StringBuilder), the call is ambiguous and won’t compile.

docs.title: Definition (Interview Style)
docs.text: A constructor is a special method used to initialize objects. It has the same name as the class and has no return type.

docs.title: Basic Example
docs.text:
class Person {
    int age;

    Person(int age) {
        this.age = age;
    }
}

When you write:

Person p = new Person(20);

JVM does:

  1. Allocate memory in heap
  2. Set default values
  3. Call constructor
  4. Assign provided values

docs.title: 📘 2️⃣ Execution Order in Inheritance (VERY IMPORTANT)
docs.text:
Consider:

class Parent {
    Parent() {
        System.out.println("Parent constructor");
    }
}

class Child extends Parent {
    Child() {
        System.out.println("Child constructor");
    }
}

When you write:

new Child();

docs.title: Output:
docs.text:
Parent constructor
Child constructor

Why?

Because:

👉 super() is automatically inserted as first line in child constructor.

docs.title: Rule (Memorize)
docs.text:
• this() calls another constructor in same class
  • super() calls parent constructor
  • Must be first line
  • Cannot use both together

docs.title: Example of this()
docs.text:
class Test {
    Test() {
        this(10);
        System.out.println("Default");
    }

    Test(int x) {
        System.out.println("Parameterized");
    }
}

When:

new Test();

Output:

Parameterized
Default

docs.title: 📘 4️⃣ Initialization Blocks
docs.text: There are two types:

docs.title: 1️⃣ Instance Initialization Block
docs.text:
{
   System.out.println("Instance block");
}

Runs: 👉 Every time object is created 👉 Before constructor body

docs.title: 2️⃣ Static Initialization Block
docs.text:
static {
   System.out.println("Static block");
}

Runs: 👉 Only once 👉 When class is loaded

docs.title: 🔥 Execution Order (VERY IMPORTANT)
docs.text:
When object is created:

  1. Static variables
  2. Static blocks
  3. Instance variables
  4. Instance blocks
  5. Constructor

In inheritance:

Parent static Child static Parent instance Parent constructor Child instance Child constructor

docs.title: 🔥 Interview-Level Trap
docs.text:
Predict output:

class Parent {
    static {
        System.out.println("Parent static");
    }

    {
        System.out.println("Parent instance");
    }

    Parent() {
        System.out.println("Parent constructor");
    }
}

class Child extends Parent {
    static {
        System.out.println("Child static");
    }

    {
        System.out.println("Child instance");
    }

    Child() {
        System.out.println("Child constructor");
    }
}

public class Test {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new Child();
    }
}

✅ Output:

Parent static
Child static
Parent instance
Parent constructor
Child instance
Child constructor

docs.title: ✅ Why This Order Happens (Interview-Level Explanation)
docs.text: When new Child() runs:

docs.title: 🔹 Step 1: Class Loading Phase (Only once)
docs.text:
1️⃣ Parent static block 2️⃣ Child static block

Static blocks run:

  • Once
  • When class is loaded
  • Parent loads before child

docs.title: 🔹 Step 2: Object Creation Phase
docs.text: Now instance-level execution begins.

docs.title: Order in inheritance:
docs.text: 3️⃣ Parent instance block 4️⃣ Parent constructor 5️⃣ Child instance block 6️⃣ Child constructor

docs.title: 🧠 Golden Rule (Memorize This)
docs.text:
When creating child object:

Parent static
Child static
Parent instance
Parent constructor
Child instance
Child constructor

docs.title: 🚨 Now Harder Trap (Very Common)
docs.text:
Predict output:

class A {
    A() {
        System.out.println("A constructor");
        show();
    }

    void show() {
        System.out.println("A show");
    }
}

class B extends A {
    int x = 10;

    B() {
        System.out.println("B constructor");
    }

    @Override
    void show() {
        System.out.println("B show " + x);
    }
}

public class Test {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new B();
    }
}

⚠ This is a famous interview trap.

docs.title: ✅ Correct Output (in order)
docs.text:
A constructor
B show 0
B constructor

docs.title: Key rule:
docs.text: ✅ Overridden methods are virtual, even inside constructors. So when A() calls show(), it actually calls B.show() because the real object is new B().

docs.title: 1️⃣ new B() starts
docs.text:
Before B constructor body runs, Java must run:

👉 super() → calls A() first

docs.title: 2️⃣ Inside A() constructor
docs.text:
System.out.println("A constructor");   // prints
show();                                // calls overridden method!

So it prints:

A constructor

Now show() call:

Because actual object is B, it calls:

@Override
void show() {
    System.out.println("B show " + x);
}

docs.title: 🚨 Trap: What is x at this moment?
docs.text:
x = 10 is an instance field initializer in class B. But B’s instance fields are initialized only after super() finishes.

So during A() execution:

  • x is still at its default value → 0

So it prints:

B show 0

docs.title: 3️⃣ A() finishes
docs.text:
Now Java initializes B instance fields:

  • x = 10

Then B() constructor body runs:

B constructor

docs.title: ✅ Final Output
docs.text:
A constructor
B show 0
B constructor

docs.title: 🧠 Interview Answer (One line)
docs.text: Calling overridable methods from constructors is dangerous because subclass fields may not be initialized yet, so the overridden method can see default values.

docs.title: 🔥 Quick check (one question)
docs.text:
If I make int x = 10; into static int x = 10; then what would show() print during A() constructor?

✅ Correct — it would print 10.

docs.title: Why (quick, interview-ready)
docs.text: static variables are initialized at class loading time, before any object is created. So when A() constructor calls show(), B.show() reads static x which is already initialized to 10.

docs.title: Notes line (very important)
docs.text:
• Instance fields may still be default (0/null) during super() execution.
  • Static fields are ready earlier (class-load phase).

docs.title: 🔥 Next constructor trap: this() vs super() rule
docs.text:
Does this compile? If not, why?

class A {
    A() { }
}

class B extends A {
    B() {
        this(10);
        super();
    }

    B(int x) { }
}

Answer: compile / not compile + reason (1–2 lines).

✅ Ouput — it does not compile.

It does not compile because a constructor must call either this() or super() as the first statement, and you cannot have both.

docs.title: Why Exactly It Fails
docs.text:
class B extends A {
    B() {
        this(10);
        super();   // ❌ compile error
    }
}

docs.title: Java Rule:
docs.text:
• If you write this(), it must be the first line
  • If you write super(), it must also be the first line
  • You cannot call both
  • You cannot place super() after this()

So compiler error:

Constructor call must be the first statement in constructor

docs.title: Important Clarification
docs.text:
When you write:

B() {
    this(10);
}

Then inside B(int x), Java automatically inserts:

super();

So the actual call chain becomes:

B() → B(int x) → super() → A()

Java ensures parent constructor runs first, but through the chained constructor.

docs.title: 🧠 Interview Rule (Memorize)
docs.text:
• Constructor can call either this() or super()
  • It must be the first statement
  • If not written, compiler inserts super() automatically

docs.title: 🔥 Final Constructor Trap (Very Common)
docs.text:
Predict output:

class A {
    A() {
        System.out.println("A");
    }
}

class B extends A {
    B() {
        System.out.println("B");
    }

    B(int x) {
        this();
        System.out.println("C");
    }
}

public class Test {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new B(10);
    }
}

Write exact output in order.

✅ Ouput:

A
B
C

Now let’s break it down clearly (interview style).

docs.title: Code
docs.text: new B(10);

docs.title: Step 1️⃣ → B(int x) constructor runs
docs.text:
B(int x) {
    this();          // calls B()
    System.out.println("C");
}

So first it calls:

docs.title: Step 2️⃣ → B() constructor
docs.text:
B() {
    System.out.println("B");
}

But before B() runs, Java automatically inserts:

super();

So it calls:

docs.title: Step 3️⃣ → A() constructor
docs.text:
A() {
    System.out.println("A");
}

Prints:

A

docs.title: Step 4️⃣ → Back to B()
docs.text:
Prints:

B

docs.title: Step 5️⃣ → Back to B(int x)
docs.text:
Prints:

C

docs.title: 🧠 Final Execution Chain
docs.text:
B(int) → this() → B() → super() → A()

And printing order:

A
B
C

docs.title: 🔥 You Now Fully Understand
docs.text:
• Constructor chaining
  • this() vs super()
  • Execution order
  • Static vs instance blocks
  • Method call inside constructor trap

That is strong OOP foundation.

docs.title: 1️⃣ What is an Instance Member?
docs.text:
Instance members:

  • Belong to an object
  • Stored in heap (inside object)
  • Each object gets its own copy

Example:

```java id="i1gk2s" class Person { int age; // instance variable } 

If you create:

```java id="z3w8tm"
Person p1 = new Person();
Person p2 = new Person();

Memory:

```id="q9a4lf" Heap: p1 → { age } p2 → { age } 

Each object has its own `age`.

---

# 2️⃣ What is a Static Member?

Static members:

* Belong to the class
* Only ONE copy exists
* Shared among all objects
* Loaded when class loads

Example:

```java id="u7dn23"
class Person {
    static int count;
}

No matter how many objects:

```java id="t4dk1x" new Person(); new Person(); 

There is only ONE `count`.

---

# 3️⃣ Memory Location (Important for Interviews)

Modern JVM (Java 8+):

* Instance variables → Heap (inside object)
* Static variables → Heap (inside Class metadata structure)
* Static methods → Method area (metadata)
* Stack → Local variables & method calls

---

# 4️⃣ Static Method vs Instance Method

## Static Method

```java id="s8v34k"
static void show() { }

  • Belongs to class
  • Cannot access instance variables directly
  • Cannot use this
  • Cannot call non-static method directly

docs.title: Instance Method
docs.text:
```java id="f1y27d" void show() { } 

* Belongs to object
* Can access both static & instance members

---

# ✅ Trap 1 — Deep Explanation

```java
class Test {
    static int x = 0;
    int y = 0;

    Test() {
        x++;
        y++;
    }
}

docs.title: After Test t1 = new Test();
docs.text:
• x = 1
  • t1.y = 1

docs.title: After Test t2 = new Test();
docs.text:
• x = 2
  • t2.y = 1

Because:

  • x is static → shared
  • y is instance → each object has separate copy

docs.title: So output:
docs.text:
2 1
2 1

docs.title: ✅ Trap 2 — Why It Does Not Compile
docs.text:
static void show() {
    System.out.println(this);
}

Why error?

Because:

  • Static method belongs to class
  • No object context
  • this refers to current object
  • Static method may be called without object

So compiler error:

non-static variable this cannot be referenced from a static context

docs.title: 🧠 Interview One-Liner
docs.text: Static members belong to the class and do not have access to instance context, therefore they cannot use this.

docs.title: 🔥 Now Slightly Harder Trap
docs.text:
Predict output:

class Test {
    static int x = 10;

    static {
        x = x + 5;
    }

    {
        x = x + 2;
    }

    Test() {
        x = x + 3;
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new Test();
        System.out.println(x);
    }
}

✅ Correct — final value is 20.

Here’s the clean dry run (interview style):

docs.title: Code flow
docs.text:
Initial:

  • static int x = 10

docs.title: 1️⃣ Static block (runs once when class loads)
docs.text:
static { x = x + 5; }

So:

  • x = 15

docs.title: 2️⃣ new Test() starts object creation
docs.text:
Instance block runs before constructor:

{ x = x + 2; }

So:

  • x = 17

docs.title: 3️⃣ Constructor runs
docs.text:
Test() { x = x + 3; }

So:

  • x = 20

docs.title: Print:
docs.text: 20

docs.title: 🧠 Notes line
docs.text: Order: static fields → static blocks → instance blocks → constructor

docs.title: 🔥 Next trap: Static method “overriding” (actually hiding)
docs.text:
Predict output:

class A {
    static void m() { System.out.println("A"); }
}
class B extends A {
    static void m() { System.out.println("B"); }
}
public class Test {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        A a = new B();
        a.m();
    }
}

✅ Correct — output is A.

Reason (interview one-liner):

Static methods are hidden, not overridden. The call is resolved at compile time based on the reference type (A), so it calls A.m().

docs.title: 📘 Static vs Instance: Final Notes Snapshot
docs.text:
• Instance members: per-object, heap, accessed via object, support runtime polymorphism (overriding).
  • Static members: per-class (one copy), available without object, no this, no overriding (only hiding), resolved at compile time.

docs.title: Next mini-trap (last for Static vs Instance)
docs.text:
Does this compile? If yes, what prints?

class Test {
    static int x = 10;
    int x = 20;

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println(x);
    }
}

Answer: compile / not compile + reason.

✅ Correct Output: — it does not compile.

It does not compile because a class cannot have two fields with the same name, even if one is static and the other is instance.

docs.title: Why Exactly?
docs.text:
static int x = 10;
int x = 20;

Even though:

  • One is static
  • One is instance

They are still fields of the same class, and Java does not allow duplicate field names in the same class scope.

You’ll get compile-time error like:

variable x is already defined in class Test

docs.title: 🧠 Important Clarification
docs.text:
You can have same variable name in:

  • Different classes
  • Parent & child class (field hiding)

Example:

class A {
    int x = 10;
}

class B extends A {
    int x = 20;
}

This compiles.

But in same class → not allowed.

docs.title: 🔥 Final Static vs Instance Master Question
docs.text:
Predict output:

class A {
    int x = 10;
}

class B extends A {
    int x = 20;

    void print() {
        System.out.println(x);
        System.out.println(super.x);
    }
}

public class Test {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        B b = new B();
        b.print();
    }
}

✅ Output:

20
10

docs.title: Why?
docs.text:
class A {
    int x = 10;
}

class B extends A {
    int x = 20;
}

This is field hiding, not overriding.

Important difference:

  • Methods → can be overridden (runtime polymorphism)
  • Fields → are resolved at compile time based on reference type

docs.title: Inside print() method
docs.text:
System.out.println(x);        // refers to B.x
System.out.println(super.x);  // refers to A.x

So:

  • x → 20
  • super.x → 10

docs.title: 🧠 Interview Insight
docs.text:
Fields are not polymorphic. They are accessed based on reference type at compile time.

Example trap:

A obj = new B();
System.out.println(obj.x);

What prints?

👉 10 (because reference type is A)

Even though object is B.
